quotes.quote: We explore the non-uniqueness of the + nucleus model in further detail for NGC 4458, in which we plot the l05 data for this galaxy extended to large radii by the addition of the B band data of jed (Figure fig:n4458 ). The Nuker law is fitted to <15'', or 2.8 decades in radius, a portion of the galaxy that cote consider to be a bright and extended nucleus. The lf fit in contrast is valid over only 4''<<50'', a little more than a single decade in radius, but is then extrapolated inwards by 1.6 decades to the radius at which lf report '.

The profile of this object is obviously complex and the Nuker residuals shown in l05 show a sinusoidal pattern, associated with an increase in profile slope at 1''. Perhaps by our criterion, this could be interpreted to be the onset of a nucleus. Even so, the Nuker law fitted to just >1'' would clearly model this region with less curvature than implied by the model, again giving a less luminous nucleus. Yet another interpretation, however, is that NGC 4458 is a dwarf S0 galaxy, in which the Nuker law appropriately describes the bulge while the law correctly captures the disk. The case of NGC 4458 emphasizes that the use of models plus large nuclei is neither unique nor compelling in galaxies of the luminosities that we are considering.

sources.summary: NGC 4458 is used as a Virgo elliptical comparison object with similar side-band H equivalent width to NGC 4467, lower CN absorption, and a complex-population fit favored over simpler models.

quotes.quote: In the top panels, we focus on NGC 4458 and NGC 4467, since these galaxies give a similar side-band H EW but very different BMC-based measurements. Looking in detail at the SEDs on the right, the red side-band of NGC4467 is more affected by increased absorption, mainly caused by the CN features which, as reported in , are stronger in NGC4467 (CN _1 =0.110 mag), than in NGC4458 (CN _1 =0.074 mag). Notice that this does not strongly affect the BMC pseudo-continuum. In addition, the H absorption observed in NGC 4467 is possibly underestimated relative to NGC 4458.

One interesting result is that the CXP models, which incorporate a spread both in metallicity and age, find solutions that are as good as -- and in the case of NGC4458, and NGC4467 and NGC 4697 significantly better than -- the other models.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4458's nuclear stellar disk, finding consistent multiband structural parameters with a 13 pc scale length and identifying it as the smallest, least luminous disk in the sample.

quotes.quote: To find the best-fit model of NGC 4458, we adjusted the disk parameters until the galaxy isophotes in the inner 1'' have a_4 0.01 instead of being perfectly elliptical (Fig. fig:plot4458 ). The scale length, inclination, and position angle of the nuclear disk measured in the ACS/F475W and WFPC2/F555W passbands are consistent within the errors. They are also consistent within the errors with the values measured by moreetal04 in the WFPC2/F814W image (Fig. fig:parameters ). The mean values of the parameters are h = 13 pc, i = 83^ , and PA = 7^ , respectively. It is the smallest and least luminous NSD ( L_ F814W = 3 10^6 ) of the sample.

sources.summary: NGC 4458 is a slowly rotating E0 galaxy with a kinematically decoupled core, declining velocity dispersion, and old stellar populations not tracing that core.

quotes.quote: NGC 4458 = VCC 1146 This slowly rotating E0 galaxy ( = 0.07) harbours a kinematically decoupled core. In the inner 1.5 arcsec, the rotation reaches V_ max = 45 at 0.7 arcsec and vanishes at about 4 arcsec. Over this radial range, the velocity dispersion decreases from 130 to 85 , and it slowly continues to decline at larger radius. Its kinetically decoupled core (KDC) was first noted by hal2001 . The stellar population parameters do not reflect the presence of a KDC.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4458 as a small Virgo elliptical with dense, low-dispersion core conditions that should destroy wider binaries and affect stellar-population IMF diagnostics.

quotes.quote: Now, let us consider two relatively extreme giant elliptical galaxies: M87, the central galaxy in the Virgo Cluster, and NGC 4458, a small galaxy in the Virgo Cluster. For M87, the central velocity dispersion is about 400 km/sec, and the central density is about 200 stars per cubic parsec (Gebhardt Thomas 2009). For NGC 4458, the central density is about 2800 L_ /pc ^3 (Gebhardt et al. 1996), and the central velocity dispersion in 85 km/sec (van Dokkum Conroy 2011). In general, the fundamental plane relation (Dressler et al. 1987) shows giant elliptical galaxies which fit on the plane will become significantly denser and have significantly lower velocity dispersions as their masses drop. For a 10 Gyr old population in a dynamical environment like that of M87, binary separations of up to about 20 AU will be possible in the core; for a galaxy like NGC 4458, binary separations of about 0.4 AU will be possible.

Later, in Conroy van Dokkum (2011), they argued further that the much smaller elliptical galaxy NGC 4458 had an initial mass function much closer to the Salpeter IMF, again on the basis of its Wing-Ford band.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4458 as the comparison case with a disentangled nuclear stellar disk, describing an old NSD and early mass assembly in a Virgo elliptical.

quotes.quote: Indeed, the only other NSD, whose stellar population was disentangled from that of its host galaxy is that in NGC 4458 Sarzi2015b . NGC 4458 is an intermediate-mass elliptical in the Virgo cluster. Its NSD is older than 6 Gyr, implying that the galaxy did not experience a disruptive event since z 0.6 . Therefore, NGC 4458 assembled most of its mass long time ago, consistently with the picture where smaller galaxies formed earlier and high-density environments reduce the incidence of mergers.

sources.summary: The paper observes NGC 4458 with Gemini/NIFS, finds central velocity-dispersion evidence for an SMBH, and flags its slow rotation and misalignment as limitations for axisymmetric modeling.

quotes.quote: We observed four of our galaxies, NGC4458, NGC4623, NGC4379 and NGC4262, using the Near-Infrared Integral Field Spectrometer NIFS, nifspaper on the Gemini-North telescope.

Galaxies NGC 4458, 4379, 4550 and 4262 show an increased velocity dispersion near the centre of the galaxy, indicative of the presence of an SMBH. The JAM models only show this rising velocity dispersion if a SMBH is included, whereas the models without a SMBH have decreasing velocity dispersions towards the centre.
However, there are galaxies within our sample that are not fully axisymmetric as assumed by the Jeans models: NGC 4458 and 4550 are both slow rotators, and additionally, NGC 4458 is misaligned.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC4458 as an elliptical with internal structure, an unusually high fitted bulge index, and no improvement when adding a PSF component to the fit.

quotes.quote: In this group we find 10 early-type galaxies (E/S0), where 9 have bulge values 2.8 - 5.7 and one, NGC4458, has n_ b =11.3 .
One case where the addition of a PSF component failed to improve the fit is the elliptical galaxy NGC4458 (see the Appendix sec:notes for more details on this galaxy).
NGC4458 E An elliptical galaxy with an internal structure.

gmw_00002018.name: NGC 4467
gmw_00002018.overview: NGC 4467 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located near the giant galaxy NGC 4472 [arXiv:1507.01336]. Its outer regions are tidally truncated by this companion [arXiv:1507.01336], showing a transition to disky isophotes [arXiv:1507.01336] and strong carbon-nitrogen absorption features [arXiv:0812.2029].

sources.summary: The paper highlights NGC 4467 as a Virgo elliptical whose CN absorption, H absorption, iron abundance, and BMC/side-band behavior demonstrate the line-index method differences.

quotes.quote: In the top panels, we focus on NGC 4458 and NGC 4467, since these galaxies give a similar side-band H EW but very different BMC-based measurements. Looking in detail at the SEDs on the right, the red side-band of NGC4467 is more affected by increased absorption, mainly caused by the CN features which, as reported in , are stronger in NGC4467 (CN _1 =0.110 mag), than in NGC4458 (CN _1 =0.074 mag). Notice that this does not strongly affect the BMC pseudo-continuum. In addition, the H absorption observed in NGC 4467 is possibly underestimated relative to NGC 4458. The iron abundance reported by is higher in NGC 4467 by 0.1 dex, an effect that would be hidden by the normalisation over the iron lines around 4070 .

In the bottom panels of figure fig:Balmer1 we investigate the H lines in NGC 4387 and NGC 4467 which have a similar EW using the BMC method, whereas they differ by 0.5 \ in the side-band method. The SEDs reveal that these two galaxies do indeed have similar Balmer absorption, but the side-band method is 'tricked' when comparing the flux with the continuum (straight line). The increased G4300 absorption in NGC 4387 pushes the pseudo-continuum down, which reduces the EW value incorrectly.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC4467 as a compact dwarf elliptical near NGC 4472, finding deep surface-brightness profiles, tidal truncation, profile deviations, and disky outer isophotes.

quotes.quote: NGC 4467 NGC4467 is a faint-system classified dwarf elliptical (e.g., Bender et al. 1992, but see also the classification as an E3 galaxy by F+06). It lies at an apparent distance of 4.2' from NGC 4472, equivalent to 23 kpc. F+06 found that its SB profile is tidally truncated in the outer regions, where the ellipticity is also affected by the close giant companion.

Our azimuthal SB profiles are shown in Fig. prof4467 . Despite the bright background of NGC 4472, flux could be measured out to a/a_ e 6.8 , where _ g 28.3 mag/arcsec ^2 and _ i 27.0 mag/arcsec ^2 . The SB profiles deviate from an ^ 1/4 profile at all radii, as shown by the (O-C) profile in the same figure.

The shape parameters (in particular b_4 , see Fig. ell4467 ) show the emergence of disky isophotes with higher ellipticity than the center in the outer bluer regions. Here there is a rapid transition from boxy to disky isophote shapes and a quite significant twisting of the isophotes of about 20 deg at a 20'' .

gmw_00002019.name: NGC 4387
gmw_00002019.overview: NGC 4387 is an E4 elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1105.4809] located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1004.4439]. It features boxy isophotes [arXiv:1105.4809], a dynamically colder center [arXiv:1105.4809], and an old stellar population [arXiv:1004.4439] that is well described by simple stellar population models [arXiv:0812.2029, arXiv:1004.4439].

sources.summary: NGC 4387 is compared with NGC 4467 in Balmer-line measurements; its stronger G4300 absorption lowers the pseudo-continuum, while SSP models adequately describe its stellar population.

quotes.quote: In the bottom panels of figure fig:Balmer1 we investigate the H lines in NGC 4387 and NGC 4467 which have a similar EW using the BMC method, whereas they differ by 0.5 \ in the side-band method. The SEDs reveal that these two galaxies do indeed have similar Balmer absorption, but the side-band method is 'tricked' when comparing the flux with the continuum (straight line). The increased G4300 absorption in NGC 4387 pushes the pseudo-continuum down, which reduces the EW value incorrectly. This is consistent with the measured absorption at this resolution of NGC4467 (G4300 _ 20 = 6.5 ) compared to that in NGC4387 (G4300 _ 20 = 7.2 ).
For some galaxies, such as NGC 4365, NGC 4387 and NGC 4473, an SSP is as good as the other models presented here and in fact the parameters recovered by complex SFHs essentially reduce to an SSP.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4387 as an old, low-velocity-dispersion Virgo early-type galaxy whose spectra are closely matched by SSP models and show interpretable line-strength gradients.

quotes.quote: Our subsample of galaxies (NGC 4387, NGC 4473, NGC 4489 and NGC 4621) cover a range in age, metallicity, abundance ratio and mass, as these galaxies were selected alongside the Colour-Magnitue Relation of the Virgo cluster. The residuals of the comparison for the low-velocity dispersion ( ), old galaxy, NGC 4387, are very small.

We plot the radial line-strength profile for the Virgo galaxy NGC 4387, which has been obtained from low resolution Keck/LRIS spectra by S anchez-Bl azquez et al. (2007). According to the V99 model grid, the observed gradient can be attributed to a decreasing metallicity with increasing galactocentric distance. Using the MILES SSP grid, the gradient can be explained by a combination of increasing age and decreasing metallicity.

sources.summary: NGC 4387 is an E4 galaxy with boxy isophotes, a dynamically colder center, changing velocity dispersion, measured rotation, and consistent GMOS/VAKU profiles.

quotes.quote: NGC 4387 = VCC 828 This E4 galaxy has boxy isophotes. It has dynamically colder centre. The velocity dispersion rises from _0 = 82 \ to = 105 at 2 arcsec, further out it decreases to 75 . The rotation velocity reaches V_ max = 47 at = 15--20 arcsec (i. e. 1 ). The profiles derived from the GMOS and VAKU observations are consistent.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4387 as the low-mass ETG comparison object, finding Kroupa-like IMF-sensitive features at all radii and almost no radial IMF dependence.

quotes.quote: img:qua the spectral region around the IMF-sensitive TiO _2 spectral feature trager is shown, for two radial bins corresponding to the centre (top) and one half of the effective radius (0.5 , bottom) for NGC 4387 (left) and NGC 4552 (right), respectively.

We find that: (i) the TiO _2 spectral region of the low-mass galaxy is well reproduced by Kroupa-like IMF models at all radii; (ii) on the contrary, for our reference high-mass galaxy, a significant TiO _2 mismatch towards a bottom-heavy IMF is detected only in the central region.
In contrast, the low-mass ETG, NGC 4387, shows almost no radial dependence of IMF within the uncertainties, with _ b =-0.3 .

gmw_00002020.name: 3C 236
gmw_00002020.overview: The giant radio galaxy 3C 236 is a massive, disturbed elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1907.09060, arXiv:2006.09388] hosting a restarted active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1907.09060, arXiv:2006.09388] with a sub-galactic inner double source [arXiv:2208.02130, arXiv:astro-ph/0702376]. It features a warped dusty disk [arXiv:1907.09060, arXiv:2006.09388], repeated bursts of star formation [arXiv:1907.09060, arXiv:2006.09388], and fast, clumpy gas outflows [arXiv:1201.1503, arXiv:1807.01475].

sources.summary: The paper uses LOFAR observations of 3C 236 to map its lobes, hotspots, spectral steepening, and host-galaxy environment as evidence for intermittent radio activity.

quotes.quote: We have examined the giant radio galaxy 3C 236 using LOFAR at 143 MHz down to an angular resolution of 7 , in combination with observations at higher frequencies. We have used the low frequency data to derive spectral index maps with the highest resolution yet at these low frequencies. We confirm a previous detection of an inner hotspot in the north-west lobe and for the first time observe that the south-east lobe hotspot is in fact a triple hotspot, which may point to an intermittent source activity. Also, the spectral index map of 3C 236 shows that the spectral steepening at the inner region of the northern lobe is prominent at low frequencies.

The host galaxy of 3C 236 has been studied in detail by RefWorks:227 , RefWorks:228 and RefWorks:51 . Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging has revealed repeated bursts of star formation (on timescales of 10^ 7 and 10^ 9 yrs) in a warped dusty disk surrounding the AGN. This suggests that the younger starburst may be connected to the AGN reactivation which produced the currently active Compact Steep Spectrum (CSS) radio source at its centre RefWorks:51 . Thus, 3C 236 is an example of a radio galaxy showing signs of multiple epochs of radio activity.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 236 as a massive disturbed elliptical GRG with a dust lane, blueshifted CO, multiple radio spectral ages, short depletion time, and repeated star formation.

quotes.quote: The host of 3C 236 is a massive elliptical galaxy with a distorted morphology, and a prominent dust lane. Its CO spectrum is blue-shifted by 300 km s ^ 1 with respect to the optical velocity Labiano2013 , and it corresponds to the peak of the HI- absorption Morganti2005 . The oldest and youngest spectral ages of different parts of GRG 3C 236 are 159 Myr and 51 Myr Shulevski19 .

Some of these galaxies are actively forming stars, and their depletion time is quite short (740 Myr and 280 Myr for Speca and 3C236, respectively), while others are rather quiescent and favour the AGN, like R1-1 and 3C326 (with t _ dep of 5.5 Gyr and 2.26 Gyr). Using observations of the Hubble Space Telescope, tremblay10 have shown bursts of repeated star formation in the host galaxy of 3C 236, which has a dusty disc around the AGN. Because 3C 236 is known to be a restarted AGN, this might be connected with the young starburst property of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper includes 3C 236 among nearby radio galaxies with fast ionized and atomic outflows, warm turbulent H2 emission, and blue-shifted mid-infrared line wings linked to jet feedback.

quotes.quote: We present mid-IR Spitzer spectroscopy of 8 nearby radio-galaxies where fast outflows of ionized and atomic gas were detected by previous optical and radio observations. Our main results and conclusions are the following: In all of the sources, we detect high equivalent width H _2 line emission from warm (100-5000 K) molecular gas, implying warm H _2 masses ranging from 10^8 to 10^ 10 M _ . The observed H _2 to-PAH luminosity ratios are all above the values predicted from photo-ionization models, and we suggest that the H _2 emission is associated with the dissipation of a fraction of the kinetic energy provided by the radio jet.

In 5 sources (3C 236, 3C 293, 3C 459, 4C 12.50 and PKS 1549-79), we securely detect blue-shifted wings (up to 3000 km s ^ 1 ) on the [ Ne ii ] (and [ Ne iii ] for the highest SNR spectra) that matches remarkably well with the blue-shifted, broad H i absorption associated with the outflow. All but one of these sources have resolved and very broad mid-IR rotational H _2 lines with FWHM 500 km s ^ 1 (and up to 900 km s ^ 1 ), compared to only 2 overall amongst the 298 AGN with such spectra in the Spitzer archive Dasyra2011 .

sources.summary: 3C 236 is presented as a restarted giant galaxy with a dust-lane velocity gradient, detected H I clouds, fast outflows near the active black hole, and clumpy outflowing gas.

quotes.quote: Also in the restarted, giant galaxy 3C 236 (see Tremblay10 for an overview of its properties) a velocity gradient is observed which, also in this case, seems to correspond to the dust-lane, the ionised gas disk and the molecular gas disk. Clouds of \ are also detected with masses of a few 10^4 \ see recently confirmed by Schulz et al. 2018 Struve12 . In both 3C 236 and 3C 293 the overall distribution and kinematics of the gas is complicated by the presence of fast \ outflows, see sec:outflows .

First of all, in some objects outflowing gas is also found very close to the active BH (a few tens of pc in projection; 3C 236 and 3C 293, Schulz18 ). Furthermore, the detected outflows appear often to be clumpy with cloud sizes of tens of pc and \ masses of the order of 10^4 . Such a clumpy medium has been traced in a few cases (e.g. \ 4C 12.50, 3C 236, 3C 293) and it supports the predictions made by numerical simulations where it is indeed the clumpiness of the medium that enhances the impact of the jet because the jet is meandering through the ISM to find the path of minimum resistance and so creating a cocoon of shocked medium from where the outflow originates.

sources.summary: The paper notes that 3C 236 has core, total power, and core dominance similar to Fermi radio galaxies, but is not a Fermi source; its high core dominance is attributed to restarted activity.

quotes.quote: As expected, among the 1FGL Radiogalaxies (RG), the majority of sources are above the Giovannini 1994's correlation (GC) having high CD which indicates small angle ( ) of view; M87 and Cen A are peculiar objects being below the GC while 3C 236 is interesting being not in the 1FGL Catalog (see Notes on sources below);
3C 236 has P _ core , P _ tot and CD similar to the 1 FGL RGs but it is not a Fermi source. In this case, the high CD is due to the restarted activities. It is like a young source.

sources.summary: The paper finds no extended diffuse halo in 3C 236 apart from a possible central-source enhancement also hinted at in lower-frequency WSRT data.

quotes.quote: GRG J1006+3454 In general, 3C 236 does not show any evidence of an extended diffuse radio halo, except for the emission enhancement outside its central source (Fig. 8, bottom). Although it looks like an artifact introduced by the merging process and the very strong central source, a slight extension towards this position is also visible in the 325 MHz WSRT map by Mack et al. (1997). The NVSS map (contours in the upper panel of Fig. 8) shows 82 of the total flux of the merged map.

sources.summary: The review describes 3C236 as one of the largest double-double giant radio galaxies, with an inner double and H I absorption interpreted as jet-ISM interaction.

quotes.quote: 3C236, one of the largest GRGs and also a DDRG, with the steep-spectrum core component resolved into an inner double (Fig. fig:3c326ims ), has been studied in detail by a number of authors e.g. Conway00,Schilizzi01,odea01 . VLBI observations of the inner 2 kpc double show that a narrow component (FWHM 50 km s ^ 1 ) of high opacity occurs at the tip of the eastern component, while much broader (FWHM 200 km s ^ 1 ) lower opacity gas covers most of the component. They interpret their results to be due to the interaction of the jet with the ISM of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 236 as a restarting source with compact inner emission, abundant dust, no CO detection, and detected H I absorption against its core.

quotes.quote: 3C 236 and 3C 293 are found to have such a pair of emission peaks well within their central compact cores (Schilizzi et al. 2001; Akujor et al. 1996). Interestingly, 3C 236, which is a re-starting source with a sub-galactic inner double source like 3C 293, as well as having abundant dust content is not detected in CO showing that molecular gas of the amount found in 3C 293 is not a necessary condition for restarting of nuclear activity. Three out of four (3C 236, 3C 293 and 4C 29.30) have been detected in HI absorption against the cores indicating presence of atomic hydrogen gas close to the centres.

gmw_00002021.name: PKS 1946+708
gmw_00002021.overview: PKS 1946+708 is a compact symmetric radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0301522] at a redshift of approximately 0.101 [arXiv:1005.2177, arXiv:astro-ph/0701619] that exhibits an optically disturbed structure [arXiv:1005.2177] and a highly bent VLBA jet [arXiv:1005.2177]. The galaxy is likely a merger remnant [arXiv:1005.2177] and features broad H I absorption across its 100 pc radio continuum source [arXiv:1807.01475, arXiv:astro-ph/0301522].

sources.summary: The paper revisits 1946 + 708 with Chandra, finds only an upper limit on its iron-line equivalent width, and does not confirm the previous Compton-thick interpretation.

quotes.quote: 1934 63 and 1946 + 708 risaliti2003 reported that 1934 63 and 1946 + 708 showed Beppo-SAX spectra typical of the Compton-thick sources with N_H> 2.5 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 , strong Fe K line, and the reflection hump. They also considered Compton-thin absorbers as a secondary explanation of the data. We have revisited both sources with Chandra . We could only obtain a 3 upper limit on the line equivalent width of <0.96 keV in 1934 63 and of <7.2 keV in 1946 + 708, assuming that the lines are unresolved ( = 10 eV) and located at the rest energies, 6.3 keV and 6.9 keV, respectively (as in risaliti2003 ). Future more sensitive X-ray observations are needed to constrain the parameters of these lines.

We measure a relatively low intrinsic absorption column density in both sources ( N_H < 2 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) and do not confirm their Compton-thick nature (see Table tab:chandra-fit ). We conclude that either the BeppoSAX spectra have been contaminated by other X-ray sources in the field, given the 2 arcmin source extraction regions in the BeppoSAX observations, or the Compton-thin model is more appropriate explanation of the data, or that these sources belong to the AGN class that switches between the Compton thick and thin states.

sources.summary: The paper classifies 1946+708 as a likely max-separation source or merger remnant with optical disturbance, a possible companion, a highly bent VLBA jet, and non-thermal colors.

quotes.quote: * 1946+708 (radio galaxy, z=0.101 ): This object appears to be disturbed in the optical. A possible companion galaxy (no redshift information available) is also observed at a projected distance of 67 kpc, thus we classify it as a max separation object. Its VLBA jet is highly bent.
In the color-color diagram, it is positioned in the non-thermal regime. This may be caused by a misclassification. That the source is a merger remnant is the most plausible alternative.

sources.summary: PKS 1946+708 is discussed as a compact double-lobed radio galaxy with H I absorption across the continuum source, consistent with a thick torus and denser outer gas.

quotes.quote: Another example is the compact, double-lobed radio galaxy, PKS 1946+708, where \ absorption was detected towards the entire 100 pc extent of the continuum source Peck99,Peck01 . The absorption against the core is broader ( 300 ), but has lower optical depth than the absorption against the rest of the source (see Fig. \ fig:1946 and Peck01 for details). According to Peck01 , this is in agreement with the thick torus scenario in which gas closer to the central engine rotates faster and is much warmer, thus lowering the optical depth. The narrow lines are explained as coming from gas further out in the torus, possibly related to an extended region of higher gas density and having a diameter of the order of at least 80 - 100 pc.

sources.summary: The paper treats S5 1946+708 as a compact symmetric radio galaxy with a 100 pc extent, broad H I absorption, and a torus whose inferred properties favor molecular gas.

quotes.quote: The second source observed with BeppoSAX is S5 1946+708, a compact symmetric object (CSO Compact symmetric objects are defined as lobe-dominated sources smaller than 1 kpc in overall size (Conway 2002) with a nucleus and two jets and an overall dimension of 100 pc. The HI absorption profile in front of the nucleus has a total width of 600-800 km s ^ 1 and the absorption in front of the jets shows that the projected thickness of the ``torus'' is about 30 pc with some narrower absorption further out (Peck et al. 1999).

In the case of S5 1946+708 we then would conclude from Table 3 that, in fact, N _ H =22.5 . From the data of Peck et al. we have N _ HI =22.5+ T_ sp,3 and thus for a high pressure atomic torus N_ HI =23.4 which is evidently impossible. So the torus should be molecular.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1946+708 as a z = 0.10083 GPS radio galaxy with elongated radio and optical structure, a marginal near-UV point source, and possible nearby group members.

quotes.quote: 1946+708 : The counterpart is identified with a z = 0.10083, m _ R =16.3 galaxy e.g., Snellen03b . VLBI observations of this GPS source show an elongated, 40 mas ( 0.07 kpc) NE-SW structure Taylor97 . Optical observations Perlman01 also suggest a NE-SW elongated source. Our near-UV image suggests a marginal detection of a point source with magnitude 24.53, present in two of the three individual pre-drizzle images. Perlman01 suggest that 1946+708 may be part of a group of galaxies and the closest would be 1 far from 1946+708 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 1946+70 as a moderate-PAH CSO lacking detectable 6.2 micron PAH emission and best fit by a specific CLUMPY dust model.

quotes.quote: 1146+59, 4C +31.04, 1946+70, PKS 1718 649, and 4C +37.11 all show moderate PAH emission strongest at 11.3 and 12.7 .
Two CSOs, 1946+70 and 4C +37.11, have no detectable 6.2 PAH emission, although the upper limit is consistent with the 6.2/11.3 band ratio of 1.1 found by smi07 for star-forming galaxies.
The best fit is for 1946+70, with Y=100 , N_0=10 , q=1.0 , and _V=10 .

gmw_00002022.name: B2352+495
gmw_00002022.overview: B2352+495 is a gigahertz-peaked spectrum radio galaxy [arXiv:0910.3611, arXiv:astro-ph/0701619] located at a redshift of approximately 0.238 [arXiv:0910.3611, arXiv:astro-ph/9907120]. It is classified as an archetypical compact symmetric object [arXiv:2303.11361] with a complex, two-sided radio morphology [arXiv:0910.3611] and an estimated kinematic age of about 1,900 years [arXiv:astro-ph/9907120].

sources.summary: J2355+4950 is treated as the archetypical CSO 2.2, with asymmetric lobes, a pinpointed nucleus, possible beaming, and evidence for evolution in its jets and lobes.

quotes.quote: J2355+4950, CSO 2.2 [ 24 ] : This object has been extensively studied as the archetypical CSO by R96, who showed that it has two extended lobes - the southern one with a hot-spot at the outer edge of he lobe, and the northern one having no hot-spot. Although the southern lobe has a hot-spot, material is spilling out of this hot-spot towards the east. In addition, at lower frequencies the structure extends outside of the jets and hot spots to form a common envelope. The position of the flat-spectrum nucleus was pinpointed by 1996ApJ...463...95T .

R96 showed that there is a bright jet component in the northern jet, and they also showed that the northern jet is an order of magnitude brighter than the southern jet. This strongly suggests that the northern side is the approaching side and that we are seeing slightly beamed radiation from the two jets. On this interpretation, the northern lobe is seen at a considerably later epoch than the southern lobe, which could explain the lack of a hot-spot in the northern lobe if the jet is no longer powering the hot-spot in the northern lobe. There is, therefore, a strong hint of evolution in the jets and lobes.

sources.summary: The paper describes IERS B2352+495 as a radio galaxy with complex compact structure, GPS classification, a redshifted host galaxy, central black-hole estimates, and X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: IERS B2352+495 (COINS J2355+4950; OZ +488) This radio galaxy has a radio power P_ 5 GHz =10^ 26.3 . It was imaged at 4.99 GHz by pearson1988 and conway1992 , who reported on a complex structure surrounding a compact core: two distinct components were found, one with a simple structure, and the other with a complex structure. Imaging at 1.67 GHz by wilkinson1994 also showed a two-sided morphology, which led the authors to classify the source as a CSO. No radio structure on scales larger than 0.2'' (7 kpc) has been detected for this source perley1982 .

The radio spectrum is convex and GHz-peaked, and the object's classification as a GPS source odea1998 was confirmed also by torniainen2007 . The source is hosted by a galaxy with redshift z=0.237 odea1991 . In the NIR band, the source is one of the few displaying a higher nucleation (lower extended emission) in the J band than in the H and K bands devries1998a .

sources.summary: The paper describes 2352+495 as a compact symmetric radio source with weak polarization, triple morphology, hotspot separation, and an inferred kinematic age of about 1900 years.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of multi-epoch global VLBI observations of the Compact Symmetric Objects: 2352+495 and 0710+439 at 5 GHz. Analysis of data spread over almost two decades shows strong evidence for an increase in separation of the outer components of both sources at a rate of 0.2 h^ 1 (for q_ =0.5 and H_ =100 h kms ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ). Dividing the overall sizes of the sources by their separation rates implies that these Compact Symmetric Objects have a kinematic age 10^ 4 years.

The radio source 2352+495 z=0.238 , lzr96 has a high radio luminosity L_ 5GHz =1 10^ 26 h^ 2 W Hz ^ 1 , rtx96 . The radio flux density is very weakly polarised and it does not exhibit a significant time variation aah92 . cpr92 revealed for the first time the triple radio morphology of 2352+495 and from analysis of multi-epoch data it was argued that the center of activity was associated with the middle component cpr92 . Later 15 GHz observations located a compact core component at the southern end of this middle component trp96 . ht 2352+495 has been observed at 5 GHz with a global VLBI array at five epochs spread over a period of 14 years.

sources.summary: B2352+495 is treated as a compact double-lobed radio galaxy with core H I absorption, a possible circumnuclear component, and narrow redshifted absorption likely tracing infalling gas.

quotes.quote: In an other double-lobed radio galaxy, B2352+495, \ absorption with a width of 100 \ has been found located against the core, and tentatively showing a velocity gradient perpendicular to the jet direction Araya10 . Also for this case, a component tracing circumnuclear material is suggested to cause the absorption. In addition, a very narrow and redshifted (about 130 ) absorption is detected and likely tracing an infalling cloud at larger distance from the nucleus.

Similar results have been found in data of the compact symmetric object B2352+495 Araya10 which shows redshifted narrow absorption. The VLBA observations reported by Araya10 were not able to localise this component, but the idea of an infalling cloud is the most likely.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2352+495 as a GPS radio galaxy at z = 0.23790, with a complex compact radio source and three near-UV point-source components in ACS data.

quotes.quote: 2352+495 : Snellen03b identify the counterpart of this GPS source e.g., O'Dea98 with a z = 0.23790, m _R = 18.2 galaxy. VLBI observations show a complex elongated 0.07 ( 0.25 kpc) source oriented NW-SE Pollack03 . Our ACS observations show three point sources in a circle about 0.5 wide, with magnitudes 24.79, 25.13 and 25.53 (slightly above the detection limit).

gmw_00002023.name: IC4247
gmw_00002023.overview: IC4247 is a gas-rich dwarf irregular galaxy located near M83 [arXiv:1103.3707, arXiv:1902.00082]. It has a high stellar density and has experienced a relatively constant star formation history, punctuated by a period of very low star formation between 400 Myr and 2 Gyr ago [arXiv:1103.3707].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC4247 as the highest-density target, gas-rich and H-detected, with 18000 CMD stars and a fairly constant star-formation history interrupted by a low-activity interval.

quotes.quote: IC4247 has the highest stellar density in our sample, with a peak stellar density of 1040 stars per 0.1 kpc ^ 2 . This galaxy is located at a deprojected distance of 280 400 kpc from M83, with a positive tidal index (see Tab infogen ). Its neutral gas content is M_ HI 3.5 10^ 7 M _ , and this galaxy is detected also in H lee07 . In the CMD in Fig. cmds , the RGB, luminous AGB stars, blue and red helium-burning stars, and some upper MS stars are clearly visible, with a total number of 18000 recovered stars.

The average SFR is 0.0061 0.0045 M _ yr ^ 1 . This galaxy formed 20 5 of its stars prior to 8 Gyr ago and 50 5 of its stars before 5 Gyr ago. IC4247 had a rather constant SFH, with a period of slightly enhanced SFR at intermediate ages ( 2 to 7 Gyr ago), and a period of very low star formation from 400 Myr to 2 Gyr ago.

sources.summary: IC4247 is used as a low-training-cluster LEGUS dwarf example where aperture corrections are poorly constrained and colors are compared against NGC4449.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:apcorrcomp presents the aperture correction histogram in the V-band for the training clusters in IC4247 (panel a) and NGC4449 (panel b), which shows that the average aperture correction is not well defined for galaxies with low numbers of training clusters. The average aperture correction in NGC4449 follows the peak of the histogram, thus recovering the aperture correction of a typical cluster. However, the histogram in IC4247 is not well determined as there are only 2 training clusters with corrections that differ by a factor of 2 ( 0.75 mag). Furthermore, since 1 of the 2 training clusters in IC4247 lies outside the allowed limits of the aperture correction histogram, the average aperture correction is based on only a single training cluster making the aperture correction highly uncertain.

Figure fig:ccplotcomp shows different color-color plots for IC4247 and NGC4449. The top panels show IC4247 (a galaxy with only 2 training clusters) while the bottom panels show NGC4449 (a galaxy with many training clusters).

gmw_00002024.name: IGR J14488--4008
gmw_00002024.overview: IGR J14488--4008 is a giant radio galaxy that features complex main lobes [arXiv:2003.09183]. These lobes show hints of jet-precession or an X-shaped structure, and the galaxy hosts a gigahertz-peaked spectrum core [arXiv:2003.09183].

sources.summary: IGR J14488--4008 is confirmed with a 325 MHz GMRT map as a giant radio galaxy with complex lobes, contaminating nearby sources, and a GPS core inconsistent with old large-scale structure.

quotes.quote: IGR J14488--4008 has been presented as a newly discovered GRG in , together with the GMRT map at 610 MHz. Here we present the GMRT map at 325 MHz from the same campaign, confirming the morphology found in . As visible in Fig. GMRT_maps (top-right panel), two sources complicate the morphological study of this target: a radio galaxy towards NE, showing a couple of lobes and a core, and another couple of point sources close to the core, 50 arcsec NW from the map center. The main lobes have a complex structure, presenting hints of an X-shaped structure (as suggested in 2015MNRAS.451.2370M ), or jet-precession signature, but deeper observations are needed to assess one scenario over the other. Finally, the source shows a GPS core 2019ApJ...875...88B , which is at odds with the large scale, most probably old, structure.

gmw_00002025.name: IGR J17488--2338
gmw_00002025.overview: IGR J17488--2338 is a giant radio galaxy that exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley II morphology with bright, predominant lobes and a core connected by fainter emission bridges [arXiv:2003.09183]. The southern lobe presents four different emitting regions, indicating a complex interaction with the intergalactic medium [arXiv:2003.09183].

sources.summary: IGR J17488--2338 is described as a recently discovered giant radio galaxy with FR-II morphology, bright lobes, a powerful core, complex lobe structure, and a restarted GPS component.

quotes.quote: IGR J17488--2338 is a recently discovered GRG in the NVSS 2014A&A...565A...2M . In our GMRT map (Fig. GMRT_maps ), a clear FRII morphology is visible, with bright, predominant lobes and a powerful core connected by fainter emission bridges along the jets. The angular resolution of these GMRT observations (16" 7", at a position angle of 23 ^ ) has a factor of three improvement with respect to the one from NVSS, allowing us to see in more detail the complex structure of the lobes and better locate the core. From a 2D Gaussian fit of the core region we estimate a deconvolved size of 10" 4", corresponding to a projected linear size of 39 16 kpc, and a integrated flux density of 19 2 mJy. The lobes structure is complex, and marginally resolved in the NVSS: the Southern lobe presents four different emitting regions, indicative of a complex interaction with the Intergalactic Medium (IGM), while the Northern one shows the typical "bullet"-like shape.

gmw_00002026.name: PKS 2331--240
gmw_00002026.overview: PKS 2331--240 is a giant radio galaxy [arXiv:1704.07385, arXiv:2303.08842] hosting a blazar-like active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1704.07385, arXiv:1805.07366] with a core-jet structure [arXiv:2003.09183]. The source exhibits high flux variability across multiple wavebands [arXiv:1805.07366, arXiv:2003.09183] and shows evidence of a highly disturbed outflow [arXiv:1805.07366, arXiv:2003.09183].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PBC J2333.9-2343 as a giant radio galaxy with controversial AGN classifications, arguing that jet realignment made it appear blazar-like.

quotes.quote: In the present work we focus on the nucleus of PBC J2333.9-2343 (also namely PKS 2331-240, z=0.0475 parisi2012 ), which is located at RA=23h33m55.2s, DEC=-23d43m41s. This AGN called our attention because it is a GRG bassani2016 that shows different and incompatible classifications when observed at different frequencies, therefore being a good candidate to host different phases of AGN activity.

The classification of the nucleus of PBC J2333.9-2343 has been controversial in the literature, being classified as a Seyfert 2 in the optical, as unobscured AGN at X-rays, and as a blazar at radio frequencies see parisi2012 . Since these classifications were obtained using data at different epochs, we obtained simultaneous coverage at X-ray, UV, optical, and radio frequencies, and found that these conflicting classifications cannot be related to variability issues Note that the only difference is that we classified it as a type 1.9 AGN in the optical. ; instead they have to be attributed to an intrinsic property of the nucleus. Our observational results show that, in this source, both the jet and the nucleus are visible, with the jet closely pointing towards us, as we still have a direct view onto the BLR. This suggests that \ is a blazar, explaining well all the classifications given at different wavebands.
The case of \ is exceptional, as the realignment of the jet made this galaxy pass from being a radio galaxy to become a blazar.

sources.summary: The paper confirms PBC J2333.9-2343 as a giant radio galaxy with a blazar-like, likely flat-spectrum radio quasar core and a small inferred jet angle.

quotes.quote: PBC J2333.9-2343 is a giant radio galaxy at z = 0.047 with a bright central core associated to a blazar nucleus. If the nuclear blazar jet is a new phase of the jet activity, then the small orientation angle suggest a dramatic change of the jet direction. We present observations obtained between September 2018 and January 2019 (cadence larger than three days) with Effeslberg, SMARTS-1.3m, ZTF, ATLAS, Swift , and Fermi-LAT, and between April-July 2019 (daily cadence) with SMARTS-1.3m and ATLAS.

The SED of the nucleus shows two peaks, that were fitted with a one zone leptonic model. Our data and modelling shows that the high energy peak is dominated by External Compton from the dusty torus with mild contribution from Inverse Compton from the jet. The derived jet angle of 3 degrees is also typical of a blazar. Therefore, we confirm the presence of a blazar-like core in the center of this giant radio galaxy, likely a Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar with peculiar properties.

sources.summary: The paper presents PBC J2333.9-2343 as a giant radio galaxy with a blazar-like nucleus, strong multiwavelength variability, broadened lines, and jet-linked ionization or outflow signatures.

quotes.quote: PBC J2333.9-2343 is a peculiar active nucleus with two giant radio lobes and a weak blazar-like nucleus at their center. In the present work we show new optical, UV, and X-ray data taken from the San Pedro M artir telescope, the New Technology Telescope, NTT/EFOSC2, and the Swift /XRT satellite. The source is highly variable at all frequencies, in particular the strongest variations are found in the broad H component with a flux increase of 61 4 \ per cent between 2009 and 2016, following the X-ray flux increase of 62 6 \ per cent between 2010 and 2016.

We also detected a broad H component in 2016, making the optical classification change from type 1.9 to type 1.8 in one year. We have also detected a broad component of the [ OIII ] 5007 line, which is blue-shifted and of high velocity, suggesting an origin from a highly disturbed medium, possibly an outflow. The line flux variability and broad widths are indicative of a jet that is, at least in part, responsible for the ionization of the BLR and NLR.

sources.summary: PKS 2331--240 is presented as a Seyfert/BL Lac-like giant radio galaxy with face-on AGN signatures, VLBI core-jet structure, high variability, and outflow evidence.

quotes.quote: PKS 2331--240: a dedicated study on this source has been presented in . Optically classified as a Seyfert 1.8 from the presence of weak broad emission lines, it shows an unabsorbed X-ray spectrum, typical of AGN seen face-on. Moreover, WISE colours classification corresponds to a BL Lac type 2014ApJS..215...14D . VLBI observations at different frequencies (8, 15, 24 GHz) revealed a core-jet structure in the nuclear region, indicative of an orientation towards the line of sight, also confirmed by the overall nuclear Spectral Energy Distribution (SED, see 2017A&A...603A.131H for a detailed analysis). Further observations in the optical and X-ray band indicated a high flux variability ( 60 in 7 years), with variable broad H _ emission and a broad [ OIII ] component, suggesting the presence of an outflow 2018MNRAS.478.4634H .

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 2331-240 as a giant radio galaxy core, using literature or archive radio measurements to evaluate spectral shape, variability, or restarting activity.

quotes.quote: PKS 2331-240 : a study of this source was presented in , showing that a Blazar-like nucleus is present, implying a dramatic change of the jet axis with respect to the GRG structure likely on the plane of the sky. In Fig. fig:1 we report the data presented in that work, in addition to the ones collected here.

gmw_00002027.name: B3 0309+411B
gmw_00002027.overview: B3 0309+411B is a giant, broad-line radio galaxy [arXiv:2005.08569, arXiv:0809.0255] that features a megaparsec-scale structure [arXiv:2005.08569, arXiv:2003.09183]. Its highly variable core [arXiv:2005.08569, arXiv:2003.09183] displays an inverted radio spectrum [arXiv:2005.08569, arXiv:2003.09183], which may indicate a newly initiated phase of radio activity [arXiv:2003.09183].

sources.summary: The paper describes B3 0309+411B as a broad-line radio galaxy with Mpc-scale structure, a variable core, compact high-frequency morphology, and evidence for a line-of-sight jet or peaked spectrum.

quotes.quote: B3 0309+411B It is a well-known broad line radio galaxy, with Mpc-scale structure, with a milliarcsecond core with a quasar-like spectrum highly varying in the radio deBruyn1989 . The core is found to have a slightly inverted radio spectrum and it is responsible for nearly 75 per cent of the total emission.

Our observations reveal a compact morphology, either slightly resolved (22 GHz) or unresolved (45 GHz), in agreement with previous studies at similar resolution at 15 GHz with VLA in C-configuration SaikiaShastriSinhaKapahiSwarup1984 , with flux densities of the order of 1 mJy. The spectral index is compatible with flat/inverted, in agreement with previous studies. We can interpret this evidence, together with the enhanced radio flux, as due to a jet closely aligned with the line of sight, as suggested by VLBI monitoring of the pc-scale jet in the MOJAVE project e.g. Lister2019 . However, considering the lower frequencies SED points in Bruni2019 , this evidence could suggest that the turnover of the spectrum is occurring at these frequencies, confirming the high-frequency peaked scenario.

sources.summary: The paper treats B3 0309+411 as a giant radio galaxy and first broad-band AGN study, finding strong reflection but an unusually small iron-line equivalent width.

quotes.quote: QSO B0241+62 and IGR J13109-5552) are well known Broad Line Radio Galaxies (see table 1 and NED, b2 and b18 ) and show a morphology with two lobes extending from the central nucleus (see for example NVSS contour maps available in NED); the extension of the lobes for B3 0309+411 and 4C 74.26 is more than 1 Mpc, hence their classification as giant radio galaxies b29,b30 . B3 0309+411 and 4C 74.26 require reflection at more than 99 confidence level, while 3C 390.3 and QSO B0241+62 at a lower confidence level (98 and 92 respectively). Despite being highly required, R is unconstrained in B3 0309+411 due to the upper bound of 2 set on the reflection; when this bound is removed, R is found to be 2.44 ^ +1.41 _ 1.17 and the constant becomes C _ 2 =2.30 ^ +1.10 _ 0.94 .

For QSO B0241+62, B3 0309+411 and IGR J13109-5552 no previous work is available and thus the analysis presented here is the first broad-band study for those 3 AGN. However, in B3 0309+411 and 4C 74.26 the observed iron line EW is too small for the reflection measured: a possible explanation resides in the scatter expected in the EW values due to a variation in the iron abundance, or to a possible anisotropy of the source seed photons which might affect the observed spectrum b42,b43 .

sources.summary: B3 0309+411B is discussed as a core-dominated Mpc radio galaxy with quasar-like spectrum, variable core, asymmetric extended jet/counter-jet, and an inverted core suggesting a new radio phase.

quotes.quote: Images from surveys and the literature In the following we discuss the morphology of the other sources, using the best available radio images from surveys or the literature: B3 0309+411B: presented in as a core-dominated Mpc-sized radio galaxy, this source shows a quasar-like optical spectrum and a strong radio flux density variability for the core ( 35 in two years), suggesting an orientation with a small angle to the line of sight. WSRT observations at 327 MHz and 608 MHz carried out by those authors show an asymmetric flux intensity between the extended jet and counter-jet, supporting this hypothesis. performed GMRT observations at 606 MHz, revealing a curved jet with an extention up to 250 kpc from the core, and then bending towards the northern hotspot. The core spectrum from is inverted, indicating a possible peak at tens of GHz, and thus suggesting an age even younger than for GPS sources. Compared to the likely old age of the Mpc structure, the core spectrum points to a new radio phase also in this source.

gmw_00002028.name: B2 1144+35B
gmw_00002028.overview: B2 1144+35B is a low-power giant radio galaxy located in a medium-compact galaxy cluster [arXiv:0708.3902, arXiv:2202.13119]. It features a variable radio core [arXiv:astro-ph/0309620, arXiv:1903.05922], a limb-brightened parsec-scale jet [arXiv:0708.4145, arXiv:astro-ph/0406116], and a high-ionization nuclear emission region reminiscent of Seyfert galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0309620].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1144 + 352 as a low-power radio galaxy and GPS source with complex jets, BL Lac-like evidence, and high-ionization Seyfert-like nuclear emission.

quotes.quote: 1144 + 352 is a low power radio-galaxy showing variable radio polarization at 8 GHz (Dennett-Thorpe March a 2000); also its arcsecond radio core is variable. This object has been identified with the Zwicky galaxy Zw 186.48 and is a known GPS source (Snellen et al. 1995).

1144 + 352 was extensively studied by several authors, using different instruments; VLA, MERLIN and VLBI data (Giovannini et al. 1999) show a complex structure over a broad range of physical scales (1 pc -- 1 Mpc) and confirm previous suggestions of superluminal motion in this source. At parsec resolution, Giovannini et al. (1999) identified the core source and two-sided jets, very asymmetric in shape and properties; from these observations, they computed a jet orientation with respect to the line-of-sight of 25 ; this is in agreement with the source properties, intermediate between an FR I galaxy and a BL Lac object. This source was considered a BL Lac candidate by March a et al. (1996); in addition, it was detected at X-ray wavelengths by ROSAT (Brinkman et al. 1995).

All these pieces of evidence suggest that this could, indeed, be a ``hidden'' BL Lac, whose non-thermal continuum is swamped by starlight, or an object with a jet at larger angles to the line-of-sight. Analysis of the optical spectrum of 1144 + 352 suggests the presence of a high ionization nuclear emission region reminiscent of those observed in Seyfert nuclei. This is in agreement with the other AGN-like characteristics of the object.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1144+352 as a low-redshift giant radio galaxy whose post-ICRF3 VLBI positions and changing jet/core brightness shifted the astrometric reference point.

quotes.quote: The radio source 1144+352 is a giant radio galaxy at low redshift ( z = 0.063 ) 2002MNRAS.329..700S that was intensively studied with VLBI imaging at cm wavelengths in the past see and references therein 2020RNAAS...4..108T . It was observed in four experiments in the 1990s (1996 Jun 7 (BB023G), 1996 Aug 7 (NAVEX S12), 1998 Mar 3 (NEOS A253), and 1999 Jan 5 (NEOS A297)) followed by an almost 20-yr gap. Only one recent experiment 2017 Mar 23, UF001E, 2021AJ....162..121H was included in the calculation of the position for the ICRF3, therefore, that break in the position was not detected in 2018.

To illustrate the substantial changes in the source structure on scales of 10 mas, we show two archival images made from data available in the Astrogeo database, one from 1996 and another from 2017 (Fig. 1144-img ). The radio core is the northwestern component 1999ApJ...522..101G . By 2017, the southestern feature, which was the brightest in 1996, became weaker than the core while they separation increased. It is reasonable to assume that, in connection with the change in the brightness distribution of 1144+352, the astrometric reference point shifted from the southestern jet component to the core.

sources.summary: The paper describes B2 1144+35B as a nearby low-power radio galaxy and diluted BL Lac candidate with peculiar FR-I structure classified among giant radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: The low power radio galaxy B2 1144+35B (hereafter referred to as 1144+35) is identified with a faint (m _ pg = 15.7) Zwicky galaxy (ZW186.48) in a medium-compact galaxy cluster at a redshift of 0.0630. In an optical study of bright, flat-spectrum radio sources, ma96 classify 1144+35 as a BL Lac candidate even though its spectrum shows H and [ NII ] emission lines co75,mo92 . From a comparison between the measured line equivalent width and the line contrast, ma96 suggest that 1144+35 could be a diluted BL Lac.

From the radio point of view, 1144+35 has a peculiar structure as discussed in detail by g99 . It is characterized by a large scale FR-I radio structure and mac98 classifies it among the giant radio galaxies. The total angular size is 13 \ corresponding to a projected linear size of 0.9 Mpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies B2 1144+35B with Zwicky galaxy ZW186.48 at z=0.0630 and describes a peculiar parsec-scale radio structure with jet, counter-jet, and limb-brightened components.

quotes.quote: The low power radio source B2 1144+35B was identified with a faint (m _ pg = 15.7) Zwicky galaxy (ZW186.48) in a medium-compact galaxy cluster at a redshift of 0.0630. From the radio point of view, 1144+35 has a peculiar structure as discussed in detail by . New observations confirm the general structure discussed in .

The parsec scale structure (Fig. 6a) is resolved in a nuclear source (C) with a short jet (D) and counter-jet structure (E). At about 25 mas from the core we have an extended jet like structure (A and B components) which is the dominant structure in the VLBI images. This jet structure is extended, clearly limb-brightened and connected to the core by a low brightness emission.

sources.summary: B2 1144+35B is discussed as a complex giant radio galaxy with multiscale observations, variable arcsecond core, disconnected lobes, possible double-double structure, and merger-linked restarting.

quotes.quote: B2 1144+35B was previously studied in detail by , making use of multiscale VLA, MERLIN, and VLBA observations (from Mpc to pc-scale). Flux density variability for the arcsec-core up to 50 , on a 20-years time scale, has been detected by those authors, and interpreted as a possible change in the external medium along the jet. Also, discontinuities between the extended lobes and the arcsec core-jets structure were found and interpreted as the presence of two different phases in the past 10 Myr, involving a possible double-double structure, the inner jets being visible at kpc-scale resolution in their VLA image. concludes that the extended, Mpc-scale, emission is a relic with an age in the range 50-90 Myr, while the kpc-scale one should be less than 10 Myr old. Finally, they suggest that a merger event could be at the origin of the restarted activity, as suggested by the boxy shape of the optical image. In Fig. maps1 (middle-right panel), we present the NVSS image where the core is well visible at the center: there are two intervening sources - a point-like one north of the core, and a radio galaxy NW of the upper-right lobe - complicating the morphology.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1144+35 as a giant radio galaxy with a limb-brightened parsec-scale jet, a short counter-jet, measured proper motions, and a two-velocity jet interpretation.

quotes.quote: 1144+35 is a giant radio galaxy which shows a strong extended jet and a short counter-jet on the pc scale (Giovannini et al., 1999). The main jet is limb-brightened with evident substructures in the external shear layer which are moving with _a 2.7 (A, B, B1, and A1 in Fig. 6). New recent observations at 8.4 GHz have allowed to measure a possible proper motion also in the counter-jet (E in Fig. 6): _ a-cj = 0.3 0.1 in the time range 1995 - 2002 with 4 different epochs (Giovannini et al. in preparation).

According to Mirabel and Rodriguez (1994) using both the jet and counter jet proper motions we can obtain directly = 30 ^ and = 0.9. From these data we can obtain a comprehensive scenario, as follows: 1) the bulk and pattern velocity are the same; 2) the shear layer Doppler factor is 2 (being 2.3) while assuming = 15 for the inner spine, its Doppler factor is 0.7 in agreement with the observed brightness distribution; 3) if the external shear layer started with the same velocity of the inner spine, its velocity decreased from 0.998c to 0.9c in less than 100 pc suggesting an intrinsic origin for the jet velocity structure.

sources.summary: The paper uses B2 1144+35 as an FR-I jet comparison: its parsec-scale radio limb brightening and slow-layer Lorentz factor motivate the synthetic-map interpretation.

quotes.quote: Moreover, VLBI observations of some object like B2 1144+35, Mrk501 and M87 show limb brightening of the jet radio emission at the parsec scale. This effect is interpreted kinematically as due to the presence of a deboosted central spine at high Lorentz factor and of a weakly relativistic external layer.

Instead the VLBI map of B2 1144+35 can be compared with our synthetic map at inclination of 60^ , although the estimated inclination Giovannini07 for this source is 33^ 7^ . One of the possible origins of this discrepancy lies in the fact that the Lorentz factor of the slow layer in B2 1144+35 is estimated to be around 2.9 (the fast spine has 15 ) corresponding to 0.94 , while in our simulations (as shown in the previous section) the slow material is subrelativistic with 0.3 -0.4 thus becoming visible at larger angles.

sources.summary: The paper discusses B2 1144+35B as a giant radio galaxy core, using literature or archive radio measurements to evaluate spectral shape, variability, or restarting activity.

quotes.quote: B2 1144+35B : multi-epoch VLA data, from 1.4 GHz to 43 GHz, for the arcsec-core of this target have been presented in . A substantial flux density variability has been found, with a steady brightening from 1974 to 1992, followed by a dimming at all frequencies from 1992 to 2006. The authors suggest that the interaction with the external medium could be the most probable scenario to explain the variability over 3 decades. In figure fig:1 we present data for all the available VLA epochs with more than two frequencies, plus the ones taken in Effelsberg showing again flux densities lower than the previous ones, confirming the decreasing trend.

gmw_00002029.name: HE 1434--1600
gmw_00002029.overview: HE 1434--1600 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0405309] hosted by an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0405309]. The host galaxy resides in a group of at least five members [arXiv:astro-ph/0405309, arXiv:2003.09183] and has likely undergone a recent collision with its nearest companion [arXiv:astro-ph/0405309, arXiv:2003.09183].

sources.summary: The paper studies the quasar host as an elliptical galaxy in a small group, with highly ionized heterogeneous ISM, disturbed gas kinematics, and evidence for a recent collision with G1.

quotes.quote: VLT on-axis optical spectroscopy of the z=0.144 radio-loud quasar \ is presented. The spatially resolved spectra of the host galaxy are deconvolved and separated from those of the central quasar in order to study the dynamics of the stars and gas as well as the physical conditions of the ISM. We find that the host of \ is an elliptical galaxy that resides in a group of at least 5 member galaxies, and that most likely experienced a recent collision with its nearest companion. Compared with other quasar host galaxies, \ has a highly ionized ISM. The ionization state corresponds to that of typical Seyferts, but the ionized regions are not distributed in a homogeneous way around the QSO, and are located preferentially several kiloparsecs away from it.
We conclude that the recent interaction between \ and its closest companion has strongly affected the gas velocity and ionization state, from the center of the galaxy to its most external parts.

sources.summary: HE 1434--1600 is the sample’s most extended giant radio galaxy, with asymmetric core-jet structure suggesting a small viewing angle and evidence for a group/merger environment.

quotes.quote: HE 1434--1600: this source is the most extended GRG of our sample, with a projected linear size of 1.8 Mpc. The NVSS image (Fig. maps1 , bottom-left panel) clearly shows an asymmetric, possibly core-jet, structure between the two extended lobes: a single-jetted morphology is typically present in radio sources with a jet axis close to the observer's line of sight, causing a relativistic Doppler-boosting that enhances the emission of the jet coming towards the observer, and dims the one of the counter-jet. This could be the case for the core-jet structure of this source, implying an inner jet axis within 45 ^ from the line of sight, while the symmetric morphology of the relaxed lobes does not suffer from this relativistic effect. The inner jet orientation at a small angle from the line of sight is also supported by the optical classification of the core (Broad Line QSO, see 2016MNRAS.461.3165B ). Finally, , through VLT optical spectroscopy, found that the host of this AGN is part of a group of at least five galaxies, and most probably underwent a collision with its nearest companion ( 4 arcsec).

gmw_00002030.name: 4C +34.47
gmw_00002030.overview: 4C +34.47 is a giant radio quasar [arXiv:2208.02130] with a double-lobed, Fanaroff-Riley II morphology [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:1008.1681]. It features a one-sided jet extending up to 380 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1008.1681] and a variable radio core [arXiv:2208.02130] viewed at a small angle to the line of sight [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:2208.02130].

sources.summary: 4C +34.47 is a giant radio galaxy with FR-II-like lobes, a one-sided inner jet, small viewing angle, very large deprojected size, and a young restarted GPS core.

quotes.quote: 4C +34.47 presents bright lobes dominating the emission in the TGSS maps (FRII morphology, Fig. maps2 , top-right panel). , through detailed VLA observations, detected a one-sided jet in the inner kpc, analogously to 4C +63.22. An estimate of the viewing angle from the jet/counterjet emission ratio and kinematic arguments led to values < 30 . This would imply a deprojected linear size in the range 1.0--3.4 Mpc, placing this source among the most extended radio galaxies. The optical classification of this source's core supports a line of sight close to the jet axis (Seyfert 1).

sources.summary: The review describes 4C34.74 as a giant radio quasar with superluminal motion, variable radio core and optical host quasar, and a 400 kpc one-sided jet.

quotes.quote: Barthel89vlbi and Hooimeyer92 found superluminal motion in GRQ 4C34.74 and also a projected misalignment angle of 5 between the large- and the small-scale jets. Over the years the radio core has been found to be variable as well as the host quasar at optical wavelengths hocuk10 . Combining VLBI-scale results with the large-scale radio emission observation for this source, hocuk10 estimated the line of sight angle to be between 53 and 57 . GRQ 4C34.74 shows a strong one-sided jet on pc to kpc scale, extending up to 400 kpc, which is one of the largest radio jets observed along with RG CGCG 049-033 bagchi07 . On VLBI scales, for the jet of GRQ 4C34.74, Barthel89vlbi and Hooimeyer92 report a knot proper-motion of 0.29 mas year ^ 1 , which gives the projected expansion speed of 3.9 .

sources.summary: The paper maps 4C 34.47 as a giant double-lobed quasar/radio source with a 380 kpc one-sided jet and old, low-luminosity FR2-like properties.

quotes.quote: Giant double-lobed radio source 4C 34.47, associated with QSO B1721+343 is mapped at arcsecond scale resolution, and the data are subsequently analyzed within the context of current models for extragalactic radio sources.
4C 34.47 displays a straight one-sided jet, measuring a record length of 380 kpc, in its double-lobed radio structure.
The global properties of 4C 34.47 do not differ from other giant (old) FR2 radio sources: it is a slowly expanding low-luminosity radio source.

sources.summary: The review highlights 4C34.47 as a giant radio quasar with a prominent one-sided large-scale jet and a superluminal nuclear jet viewed within 44 degrees.

quotes.quote: Two of the GRQs with prominent jets are 4C34.47 Barthel89vlbi and 4C74.26 riley89 . 4C34.47 has a prominent large-scale jet without any counter jet with the flux density ratio > 10. VLBI observations of the core show a nuclear jet exhibiting superluminal motion indicating that this component lies within 44 ^ to the line of sight Barthel89vlbi .

gmw_00002031.name: 4C +74.26
gmw_00002031.overview: At a redshift of z = 0.104, 4C +74.26 is a giant radio-loud quasar hosted by an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1602.07896, arXiv:0705.1339]. It features a one-sided relativistic jet [arXiv:1602.07896, arXiv:0705.1339] and exhibits multiwavelength variability, including correlated optical-radio fluctuations and variable X-ray absorption [arXiv:1607.01943, arXiv:1805.06957].

sources.summary: The paper models 4C74.26 as a giant FR II radio quasar whose southern hotspot emission implies magnetic-field amplification, rapid downstream decay, and inefficient ultra-high-energy particle acceleration.

quotes.quote: The FR II radiogalaxy 4C74.26 is located at redshift z = 0.104 ( 0.5 Gpc from Earth) Throughout the paper we use cgs units and the cosmology H_0 = 71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _0 = 1 and _0 = 0.73 . One arcsecond represents 1.887 kpc on the plane of the sky at z = 0.104 . . This source is the largest known radio quasar, with a projected linear size of 1.1 Mpc and it has a one-sided jet. Two X-ray sources have been detected with the Chandra satellite in the termination region of the South jet (Erlund et al. 2007).

We model the radio to X-ray emission in the southern hotspot of the FR II radiogalaxy 4C74.26. Our study is based on three key features: The MERLIN emission region is too thin to be the result of fast synchrotron cooling. The radio to IR spectrum ( = 0.75 ) is too flat for the emitting volume to be determined by synchrotron cooling through this wavelength range. The turnover of the synchrotron spectrum at IR/optical frequencies requires D D_ Bohm for any reasonable shock velocity.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 4C +74.26 as a heavily X-ray absorbed radio-loud quasar, finding Galactic and intrinsic absorption, a photoionized outflow, and weak wind feedback relative to the jet.

quotes.quote: Here, we present the analysis of the X-ray absorption in the radio-loud quasar 4C +74.26. With the aim of characterizing the kinetic and the ionization conditions of the absorbing material, we fitted jointly the XMM- Newton Reflection Grating Spectrometer (RGS) and the Chandra High Energy Transmission Grating Spectrometer (HETGS) spectra, which were taken 4 months apart. The intrinsic continuum flux did not vary significantly during this time lapse. The spectrum shows the absorption signatures (e.g., Fe-UTA, O vii , and Ne vii - Ne x ) of a photoionized gas outflow ( 3.5 10^ 21 , 2.6 , v_ out 3600 ) located at the redshift of source.

We have presented a joint analysis of the RGS and HETGS spectra of the heavily X-ray absorbed radio-loud quasar 4C +74.26. Thanks to the high spectral resolution of these grating spectra, we were able to reveal a rich spectrum of absorption features originating from both Galactic and intrinsic material.

Thus, the case of 4C +74.26 confirms that the jet is a more likely driver of AGN feedback in radio-loud galaxies tor2012 . We performed a joint analysis of the RGS and HETGS spectra of the radio-loud quasar 4C +74.26.

sources.summary: The paper reports 4C +74.26 as a new NuSTAR cut-off variation source, with spectra ratios and separate observations indicating higher cut-off energies in observations B and D.

quotes.quote: With the help of this new technique, we revisit their E _c variations and report the detection of E _c variation in a new source, 4C +74.26 at z=0.1040 Riley1989 . Discussion and conclusions are given in sect:discussion .

4C +74.26: presented spectra fitting to time-averaged NuSTAR and Swift spectra of 4C +74.26. They reported clear detection of high energy cut-off of 183 ^ +51 _ 35 keV, indicating its X-ray emission is dominated by corona emission instead of jet. In this work, the spectra from 4 NuSTAR observations were analyzed separately, and simultaneous Swift spectra (see Table 3) are included. In Fig. ratio , we show the spectra ratios of 4C +74.26 between each two observations (the brighter spectrum divide the fainter one, hereafter the same for simplicity). Upward or downward curvatures in the plots are seen at high energies, suggesting possible E _c variations between exposures.
The variations of the best-fit E _c between observations are also consistent with what the spectra ratio plots have illustrated, that Obs. B D have higher E _c comparing with Obs. A C.

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C 74.26 as a giant radio-loud quasar with a luminous, offset X-ray hotspot, using radio, X-ray, and optical data to test synchrotron and inverse-Compton models.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of an X-ray counterpart to the southern radio hotspot of the largest-known radio quasar 4C 74.26 (whose redshift is z=0.104 ). Both \ and \ images reveal the same significant ( 10 , i.e. 19 ) offset between the X-ray hotspot and the radio hotspot imaged with MERLIN. The peak of the X-ray emission may be due to synchrotron or inverse-Compton emission.

4C 74.26, at redshift z=0.104 , has the morphology of a typical \ radio source FR , but is on the borderline between \ and \ in terms of its radio luminosity rileywarner . It has a one-sided jet, which lies at an angle < 50^ o to the line-of-sight pearson92 and may be indicative of beaming ( 0.5c , rileywarner ). The southern radio hotspot on the jet side is much brighter and more compact than the hotspot complex on the non-jet side, which is typical of such sources (e.g. laing89 ; bridle94 ). Optical observations show its host to be a typical giant elliptical galaxy riley88 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 4C +74.26 in a high-flux X-ray state, finding variability, spectral hardening, a broadened Fe line, and constraints on the accretion-disc inner radius.

quotes.quote: We report on a 90 ks observation of the radio-loud quasar 4C +74.26. The source was observed in its highest flux state to date, and we find that it brightened by about 20 per cent during the observation. We see evidence of spectral hardening as the count rate increases and also find that the rms variability increases with energy up to about 4 keV.

4C +74.26 is a broad line radio galaxy (BLRG) located at z=0.104 (Riley et al. 1989). Its radio luminosity is on the boarder of FRII and FRI but its morphology is clearly that of an FRII object (Riley et al. 1989). A one-sided jet which is at least 4 kpc long has been observed with the VLA (Riley Warner 1990), and on parsec scales with VLBI (Pearson et al. 1992).

sources.summary: The paper resolves 4C74.26's southern hotspot into X-ray peak and arc components and emphasizes its low-luminosity radio structure and unusually offset radio and X-ray peaks.

quotes.quote: New Chandra observations have resolved the structure of the X-ray luminous southern hotspot in the giant radio quasar 4C74.26 into two distinct features. The nearer one to the nucleus is an extremely luminous peak, extended some 5 kpc perpendicular to the orientation of the jet; 19 kpc projected further away from the central nucleus than this is an arc having similar symmetry. This X-ray arc is co-spatial with near-IR and optical emission imaged with Gemini, and radio emission imaged with MERLIN.

Like many giant radio sources, 4C 74.26 has a low luminosity \ structure. Contrary to expectation, our initial study of 4C 74.26, a radio-loud quasar at z=0.104 , illustrated that the brightest X-ray hotspot component does not necessarily trace the location of the brightest radio feature ( erlund07 ; see also hardcastle07, goodger08, evans08 ). Our initial study of the southern hotspot of 4C 74.26 showed it to have the largest known offset between its radio and X-ray peak.

sources.summary: The paper models particle acceleration in the southern hotspot of the FR II radio quasar 4C74.26, linking compact radio emission, magnetic-field amplification, and weak ultra-high-energy proton acceleration.

quotes.quote: We model the emission from radio to X-rays in the southern hotspot of the FR II source 4C74.26 using data provided in . We determine that the compact radio emission traces out the location of the shock where the magnetic field B is amplified by plasma instabilities up to 100 G, and it damps rapidly downstream of the shock. The turnover in the synchrotron spectrum between infrared (IR) and optical wavelengths implies that the CR electron scattering length is much longer than the Larmor radius and consistent with the amplified magnetic field being structured on very small scales and comparable with the ion skin-depth / _ pi . Cosmic ray acceleration is consequently very slow and electrons are accelerated to only E_ e, max 0.3 TeV.

The FR II galaxy 4C74.26 is located at redshift z = 0.104 ( 0.5 Gpc from Earth) Throughout this paper we use the cosmology H_0 = 71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _0 = 1 and _0 = 0.73 . One arcsecond represents 1.887 kpc on the plane of the sky at z = 0.104 .

sources.summary: The paper studies the radio-loud quasar 4C +74.26, linking its radio jet, accretion disk, X-ray corona, giant lobes, and delayed optical-radio variability to disk-jet coupling.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: J2000.0 coordinates: R.A.= 20^ h 42^ m 37 3 and Dec.= +75 08 02 5

quotes.quote: 4C +74.26 (J2000.0 coordinates: R.A.= 20^ h 42^ m 37 3 and Dec.= +75 08 02 5 ), introduced by Riley1988 , is one of the largest known radio sources associated with an active nucleus classified spectroscopically as a broad-line radio galaxy/quasar. It displays giant radio lobes of the FR II type Fanaroff1974,Saripalli1996,Schoenmakers2001,Lara2001,Konar2004,Machalski2011 , with the total angular size of 10 at the redshift of z=0.104 Riley1988 , yielding a projected linear size of 1.01 Mpc for the modern cosmology.

Here we explore the disk-jet connection in the broad-line radio quasar 4C+74.26, utilizing the results of the multiwavelength monitoring of the source. The target is unique in that its radiative output at radio wavelengths is dominated by a moderately-beamed nuclear jet, at optical frequencies by the accretion disk, and in the hard X-ray range by the disk corona. Our analysis reveals a correlation (local and global significance of 96 and 98 , respectively) between the optical and radio bands, with the disk lagging behind the jet by 250 42 days. We discuss the possible explanation for this, speculating that the observed disk and the jet flux changes are generated by magnetic fluctuations originating within the innermost parts of a truncated disk, and that the lag is related to a delayed radiative response of the disk when compared with the propagation timescale of magnetic perturbations along relativistic outflow.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes four XRT/NuSTAR observations of 4C 74.26, finding lower cut-off energies than prior studies even after trying ionized-reflection and warm-absorber modeling.

quotes.quote: In the case of 4C 74.26 we found four simultaneous XRT/NuSTAR observations and we analysed each data set individually, as well as their sum in a similar way as done in Lohfink_2017 .

Finally, a comparison with previous NuSTAR studies of 7 sources in the sample indicates a perfect match with our results, except for the case of 4C 74.26, where the cut-off values reported in the literature Lohfink_2017 are higher than what we find in this work. Interestingly, Malizia et al. (2014) report similar values as those obtained by Lohfink_2017 . This source is known to host a photoionised outflow Di_Gesu_2016 and both Lohfink_2017 and Malizia:2014 took this component into account in their modelling of the source, while we were unable with the XRT data to reproduce it. Furthermore Lohfink_2017 employ a different broad-band model ( relxilllp ) to fit the source spectrum. We have therefore re-analized our data to take into account both ionized reflection and warm absorber in the fit, but the high energy cut-off resulted always in a lower value, either in the individual observations or in their sum, in agreement with our initial analysis.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 4C 74.26 as a giant, uncertain radio galaxy with pile-up treatment, a possibly broad iron line, strong reflection, and anomalously low iron-line strength.

quotes.quote: QSO B0241+62 and IGR J13109-5552) are well known Broad Line Radio Galaxies (see table 1 and NED, b2 and b18 ) and show a morphology with two lobes extending from the central nucleus (see for example NVSS contour maps available in NED); the extension of the lobes for B3 0309+411 and 4C 74.26 is more than 1 Mpc, hence their classification as giant radio galaxies b29,b30 . These double-lobed objects are classified in the literature as FR II galaxies, with the exception of 4C 74.26 and S5 2116+81, which still have an uncertain/unknown nature. The line is narrow in all of our sources (hence the line width has been fixed to 10 eV), except in 4C 74.26, in which a quite strong and possibly broad iron line is detected (see also b2 and b58 ).
