The excellent quality of these data make it suitable for testing the generality of our model. Quirrenbach et al.'s observations reveal two galaxies separated by 8 kpc, the brighter of which has a diffuse, clumpy morphology, while the fainter is compact and is identified with the source of the radio jets.

Applying the model of Willott et al. \ (1999) to 3C 294, we derive a jet power Q = 1.5 10^ 40 ( )^ 31/28 W, and an age of t = 4.1 10^6 ( )^ 43/28 yr. This much younger age is consistent with the smaller separation of the two optical components in 3C 294, and the (rest-frame) optical properties of this system are then completely analogous to those of 3C 356 at an earlier epoch.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 294 as a massive z=1.786 radio galaxy with double-hotspot morphology, subclumps in the process of merging, and a surrounding overdensity of faint red galaxies.

quotes.quote: 3C 294 is a radio source associated with an emission-line galaxy, with a double-hotspot radio morphology suggestive of precessing jets originating from the weak flat-spectrum core mccarthy90 . The galaxy is embedded in a elongated Ly nebula roughly aligned with the radio structure mccarthy90 . A bright ( V=11 5 ) star 11 west of 3C 294 has hampered deep optical observations of the surrounding field, but has allowed the use of adaptive optics for deep, high-resolution imaging of the radio galaxy at near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths stockton1999,quirrenbach2001 . These observations resolve the radio galaxy in several distinct subclumps in the process of merging, supporting the idea that 3C 294 is located in a dense environment. In this Letter we present NIR imaging of the field around 3C 294 revealing intriguing evidence for an overdensity of faint red galaxies in the vicinity of 3C 294.

sources.summary: This high-redshift radio galaxy is detected as a weak extended HRI source, consistent with previous PSPC work but too faint for reliable radial profiling.

quotes.quote: 3C 294 This high-redshift radio galaxy was detected in PSPC observations by Crawford Fabian (1996a). The counts we measure from a long archival HRI observation are consistent with the result of Crawford Fabian within the errors. The source appears extended, but the low count rate prevents us from extracting a reliable radial profile. Dickinson \ (1998) reach similar conclusions.

sources.summary: The review describes 3C 294 as a z~1.8 radio galaxy surrounded by a giant Lyman alpha nebula aligned with the inner radio source and showing extended C IV and He II emission.

quotes.quote: Few years later, McCarthy et al. (1990) reported the discovery of the first giant Ly nebula extending over 120 kpc around the z 1.8 radio galaxy 3C 294. This nebula is highly elongated and well aligned with the inner radio source axis. Extended C IV and He II emission was also detected.

sources.summary: The paper cites Chandra work on 3C 294 showing a 100 kpc diffuse X-ray region, with most emission attributed to inverse-Compton scattering rather than a fully thermal intracluster medium.

quotes.quote: fabian03 performed a very deep (200 ks) study of the radio galaxy 3C 294 at z=1.79 . They observed a 100 kpc region of diffuse emission bounded by sharp edges. Although some thermal component from the ICM could not be ruled out, most of the emission was ascribed to Inverse Compton (IC) scattering of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) by an older population of electrons tracing out an hourglass-shaped region around the radio source.

gmw_00002052.name: UM287
gmw_00002052.overview: UM287 is a radio-quiet, UV-luminous quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.28 [arXiv:2012.04675, arXiv:1612.00491]. The quasar illuminates a giant Lyman-alpha structure known as the Slug Nebula [arXiv:1612.00491] and is associated with a molecular gas mass of approximately 24 billion solar masses [arXiv:2012.04675].

sources.summary: The paper detects CO(3-2) emission from UV-luminous quasar UM287, measures a CO redshift consistent with optical/UV estimates, and infers molecular gas associated with the quasar.

quotes.quote: In the Slug nebula, we clearly detect CO(3-2) emission at S/N 7 associated with the UV--luminous quasar UM287.

The CO--based redshift of UM287 ( z = 2.2824 0.0003 ) is in excellent agreement with previous estimates based on rest-frame optical/UV lines leibler18,cantalupo19 , and slightly higher than the one reported by hewett10 ( z = 2.279 ). The line-of-sight velocity difference between the quasar and the other CO--emitting galaxy in the Slug is 1850 , hinting at a physical association of the galaxy with the overdensity hosting the ELAN, although we note that the velocity difference is larger (by a factor 2 ) than the velocity gradient observed in the nebula based on emission leibler18 .

Under these assumptions, we infer molecular gas masses of (24 3) 10^9 , (13 3) 10^9 , and (48 4) 10^9 for the quasar in the Slug, the galaxy on its eastern side, and for the CO--detected galaxy at the northern end of the Jackpot ELAN, respectively.

sources.summary: The review describes UM287 as a radio-quiet quasar illuminating the 460 kpc Slug Nebula, whose size and high surface brightness require unusually clumpy intergalactic gas.

quotes.quote: Surprisingly, the first quasar observed at Keck/LRIS, i.e. UM287, showed clear evidences of extended emission over scales larger than 10" after the first 20 minutes exposure was obtained. At the end of the total integration of 10 hours and detailed data reduction, a giant Ly nebula extending to about 55" ( 460 kpc) was found around this quasar (Cantalupo et al. 2014), see Fig. 1, and named "Slug Nebula" (given its morphology and in honor of the mascot of the University of California, Santa Cruz). This discovery was surprising for several reasons: i) despite its association with a radio-quiet quasar, it is at least twice as large as any previously detected Ly Nebulae including the much more common radio-galaxy halos and Ly blobs as discussed below; ii) given its size, it extend well beyond the viral halo of the quasar into the intergalactic medium; iii) it shows a very high Surface Brightness over very large scales that cannot be easily explained unless a large gas clumping factor within intergalactic gas is invoked (see section subsec:3.1 ).

sources.summary: Q0052+0104 is unresolved but lies in a field with a much brighter CO companion, extended companion CO emission, and several continuum emitters.

quotes.quote: Q0052+0104 : This QSO is unresolved, and its field contains a companion A (CO emitter) that is five times brighter in CO(4-3), located at a projected distance of 240 kpc from the QSO. This distant companion reveals extended CO emission across 40 kpc in NE-SW direction (Fig. fig:spectrum4 ). The continuum image taken with the ALMA 12m array reveals an unresolved point source associated with the QSO and another two marginally resolved emitters at a projected distances of 16 and 190 kpc from the QSO.

gmw_00002053.name: CR7
gmw_00002053.overview: Cosmos Redshift 7 (CR7) is a highly luminous Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy located at a redshift of z = 6.6 [arXiv:1502.07355, arXiv:1904.11185]. The system is composed of three distinct components, which may host either a direct-collapse black hole, a low-mass active galactic nucleus, or a young, low-metallicity starburst [arXiv:1609.00727, arXiv:1702.00407].

sources.summary: The paper simulates CR7 as a high-redshift Lyman-alpha source powered by an accreting massive black hole, reproducing observed line luminosities and predicting detectable synchrotron emission.

quotes.quote: We present the first ab initio cosmological simulations of a CR7-like object which approximately reproduce the observed line widths and strengths. In our model, CR7 is powered by a massive ( 3.23 10^7 M_ ) black hole (BH) the accretion rate of which varies between 0.25 and 0.9 times the Eddington rate on timescales as short as 10 ^3 yr. Our model takes into account multi-dimensional effects, X-ray feedback, secondary ionizations and primordial chemistry. We estimate Ly- line widths by post-processing simulation output with Monte Carlo radiative transfer and calculate emissivity contributions from radiative recombination and collisional excitation. We find the luminosities in the Lyman- and He II 1640 angstrom lines to be 5.0 10^ 44 and 2.4 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , respectively, in agreement with the observed values of > 8.3 10^ 43 and 2.0 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 .

We also find that the black hole heats the halo and renders it unable to produce stars as required to keep the halo metal free. These results demonstrate the viability of the BH hypothesis for CR7 in a cosmological context. Assuming the BH mass and accretion rate that we find, we estimate the synchrotron luminosity of CR7 to be P 10^ 40 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , which is sufficiently luminous to be observed in Jy observations and would discriminate this scenario from one where the luminosity is driven by Population III stars.

sources.summary: The paper uses CR7 as a spectroscopic and photometric comparison LAE, finding COLA1 brighter while reporting CR7's filter placement, line width, equivalent width, and diffuse size.

quotes.quote: We compare the photometric properties of COLA1 and CR7 in Table 1 based on the present data. Both CR7 and COLA1 lie at the long-wavelength edge of the NB921 filter and we must allow for the falling transmission in the filter when converting the narrow-band magnitude to a flux. Allowing for the relative transmission factors, the imaging data shows COLA1 to be about 20 more luminous than CR7.

The measured FWHM, corrected for the instrumental resolution, is 194 km s ^ 1 in COLA1 compared to 247 km s ^ 1 in CR7. ( sobral15 give a Gaussian-fitted FWHM of 266 15 km s ^ 1 for CR7.) Only the red side of COLA1 lies above the half maximum so the FWHM relates to this component only. The measured rest-frame equivalent width of COLA1 is 53 \ and that of CR7 99 , where in both cases we have measured the continuum to redward of the line.
This contrasts with CR7 which is quite diffuse sobral15 . The present data gives an intrinsic FWHM of 1.3^ for CR7.

sources.summary: The paper identifies CR7 as a spectroscopically confirmed z=6.6 LAE in COSMOS, brighter than Himiko and detected in broad-band and IRAC data.

quotes.quote: This match is our brightest candidate, nicknamed `` COSMOS REDSHIFT 7; CR7 " . After these steps, we find 15 Lyman- candidates in COSMOS, of which two remarkably bright sources - brighter than Himiko and show their positions in Fig. fig:radec .

CR7 is detected at only half of the narrow-band filter transmission, leading to a 2" luminosity of 5.8 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , which is a factor 2 higher than our estimate from the photometry. Based on MAG-AUTO , the luminosity is 9.6 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , and thus a factor 2.5 brighter than Himiko . This is a lower limit since the COSMOS NB921 observations are shallower than in UDS and might therefore miss some lower surface brightness regions and it assumes the z band continuum being flat.

It is also detected in IRAC, with a blue [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] colour, consistent with the [ 3.6 ] m flux being boosted by strong H / [ OIII ] line-emission e.g. Smit2015 . Because of these broad-band detections, the source can be selected as a Lyman-break galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper includes CR7 as a bright, spectroscopically confirmed z=6.604 Lyman-alpha emitter with multiple HST components, reduced archival WFC3 imaging, and comparison value for bright z~7 galaxies.

quotes.quote: At z 7 the morphology of the brightest LBGs is uncertain due to the lack of high-resolution near-infrared imaging of these rare galaxies, however there have been hints of highly clumpy systems from the detailed study of two extremely bright Lyman- emitters (LAEs) nicknamed `Himiko' Ouchi2009 and `CR7' Sobral2015 . Both of these LAEs are also bright in the rest-frame UV continuum ( m_ AB 25 ), where they appear to consist of multiple components in high-resolution HST /WFC3 imaging, suggestive of a merging system Ouchi2013 . Despite the intense study of these galaxies, with only two objects it is unclear whether the extended clumpy morphology observed is typical for all bright star-forming galaxies at z 7 or is exclusive to strong Lyman- emitters.

The object was followed-up spectroscopically by Sobral2015 , who selected it based on narrow-band photometry, revealing the object to be a strong Lyman- emitter at z_ spec = 6.604 . As presented in Sobral2015 , `CR7' has been imaged by HST /WFC3 serendipitously, in a program designed to target massive star-forming galaxies at z = 2 (PI: F o rster Schreiber, ID 12578). The imaging consisted of one orbit in the YJ_ 110 band, and two in the H_ 160 band, centred on object ZC401925.
The LAE `CR7' was initially selected in Bowler2012 and again in the deeper UltraVISTA DR2 data in Bowler2014 , and has been confirmed to be a strong narrow-band emitter by Sobral2015 .

sources.summary: The paper models the z 6.6 Ly emitter CR7, arguing its updated photometry remains consistent with a Compton-thick direct-collapse black hole scenario.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, have suggested that a z 6.6 object named COSMOS redshift 7 (CR7), the brightest Ly emitter discovered so far Sobral_2015,Matthee_2015 , could be powered by a DCBH with an initial mass 10^5 M_ . In fact, the expected DCBH Spectral Energy Distribution (SED) nicely reproduces the peculiar spectrum of CR7 in its component A (see Sobral_2015 ). In addition, the DCBH model accounts for the (i) very strong Ly ( 8.5 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 ) and He 1640 ( 2.0 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 ) emission lines, and the (ii) non-detection (within the instrumental sensitivity) of metal lines. Finally, such a model is also consistent with the X-ray upper limit set by on this source.

As alternative explanations, they propose that CR7 can be classified as a more standard low-mass, narrow-line AGN (which would also explain the lack of radio and X-ray emission) or a young, low-metallicity ( 2 10^ 3 ) starburst with the presence of binaries. In this Letter , we show that the updated photometry of CR7 is well explained by the DCBH model presented by . Moreover, we provide constraints on the physical properties of this object, as the DCBH mass, accreting gas column density and metallicity.

sources.summary: The chapter discusses CR7 as a luminous z=6.6 Ly-alpha galaxy with unusually strong Ly-alpha and He1640 emission, possible direct-collapse black hole, and Population III interpretations.

quotes.quote: There has been some speculation that we may have already detected such a newly formed massive black hole: CR7 (Cosmos-Redshift 7, Sobral ) is one of the most luminous ( L_ 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ) Ly emitting galaxies in the Universe, and at z 6.6 . In addition, the Ly line is unusually strong, with a rest-frame equivalent width of EW _ 200 .

In addition, CR7 appears to have a strong He1640 line, which is expected if CR7 harbors a source of hard ionizing radiation, such as an accreting black hole. CR7 further consists of several components, one of which consists of an older, passive galaxy.

Strong Ly , He1640 line emission, combined with the blue continuum of one of the three components of CR7 has also been associated with Population III star formation (see e.g. Sobral,Visbal16 ). However, recent theoretical studies indicate that the required mass of Population III mass that is required to explain CR7 exceeds the maximum mass that is considered theoretically reasonable Visbal17,Xu16 . Recent observations by Bowler17 imply that the strength of the He1640 is weaker, and that [ OIII ] may have been detected, which would rule out the Population III scenario (but not the DCBH-scenario, see Pacucci17 ).

sources.summary: The paper reassesses CR7, finding IRAC colors inconsistent with Pop III or pristine DCBH models and favoring either a narrow-line low-mass AGN or a young low-metallicity starburst.

quotes.quote: The z = 6.6 Lyman- emitter `CR7' has been claimed to have a Population III-like stellar population, or alternatively, be a candidate Direct Collapse Black Hole (DCBH). In this paper we investigate the evidence for these exotic scenarios using recently available, deeper, optical, near-infrared and mid-infrared imaging.

We find strong Spitzer /IRAC detections for the main component of CR7 at 3.6 m and 4.5 m , and show that it has a blue colour ( = -1.2 0.3 ). This colour cannot be reproduced by current Pop. III or pristine DCBH models.

We find that a low-mass, narrow-line AGN can reproduce the observed features of CR7, including the lack of radio and X-ray detections. Alternatively, a young, low-metallicity ( 1/200 Z _ ) star-burst, modelled including binary stellar pathways, can reproduce the inferred strength of the line and simultaneously the strength of the observed OIII emission, but only if the gas shows super-solar element abundances (O/Fe 5 (O/Fe) _ ).

sources.summary: The paper models CR7 as a z 6.6 Lyman-alpha system whose component A can form a direct-collapse black hole before metal pollution from components B and C becomes prohibitive.

quotes.quote: In this study we focus on the z 6.6 Lyman- CR7 consisting of clump A that is host to a potential direct collapse black hole (DCBH), and two metal enriched star forming clumps B and C. In contrast to claims that signatures of metals rule out the existence of DCBHs, we show that metal pollution of A from star forming clumps clumps B and C is inevitable, and that A can form a DCBH well before its metallicity exceeds the critical threshold of 10^ 5 10^ 6 \ Z_ . Assuming metal mixing happens instantaneously, we derive the metallicity of A based on the star formation history of B and C.

Recent observations of the bright Ly emitter, CR7, at z 6.6 S15 hereafter Sobral15a have sparked a series of studies that aim at exploring its nature. The system is composed of three components: A, B and C, where B and C are thought to host metal-poor (Population II, Pop II) stars, while the composition of A is still under debate. Close to 90 of the flux can be attributed to component A which also appears to be extremely blue and metal-free.

sources.summary: CR7 is discussed as an extended z=6.6 COSMOS source whose narrow Ly and He II lines, components, metal enrichment, and possible direct-collapse or starburst nature remain debated.

quotes.quote: The source, CR7, is found in the COSMOS field with a redshift z = 6.6 ( 2015MNRAS.451..400M ) and is spatially extended ( 16 kpc; 2015ApJ...808..139S ). Its Ly and He II lines are narrow (FWHM 200 km s ^ 1 ), which disfavors their origin from an AGN or Wolf-Rayet stars, and no metal lines are detected. This initially suggested that CR7 could host a population of Pop III stars.

However, the finding that CR7 is formed by a blue galaxy (component A) lying close to two redder galaxies (components B and C) that could provide the Lyman-Werner radiation necessary to suppress star formation in component A seems to favor the direct collapse scenario (see Fig. CR7 ; 2015ApJ...808..139S ; 2015MNRAS.453.2465P ; 2016MNRAS.460.4003A ; 2016ApJ...823...74D ; 2016MNRAS.462.2184H ; 2016MNRAS.460.3143S ). Using deeper IR observations, found evidence for metal enrichment in component A and claimed that this rules out the presence of Pop III stars or a pristine direct collapse BH. Further observations are required to find more sources like CR7 and better constrain their nature and the conditions under which they form.

sources.summary: The paper argues that CR7 is a z = 6.6 system whose source A may host a direct-collapse black hole, enabled by Lyman-Werner radiation from sources B and C.

quotes.quote: We propose that one of the sources in the recently detected system CR7 by Sobral et al. (2015) through spectro-photometric measurements at z = 6.6 harbors a direct collapse blackhole (DCBH). We argue that the LW radiation field required for direct collapse in source A is provided by sources B and C. By tracing the LW production history and star formation rate over cosmic time for the halo hosting CR7 in a CDM universe, we demonstrate that a DCBH could have formed at z 20 . The spectrum of source A is well fit by nebular emission from primordial gas around a BH with MBH 4.4 10^6 accreting at a 40 of the Eddington rate, which strongly supports our interpretation of the data. Combining these lines of evidence, we argue that CR7 might well be the first DCBH candidate.

sources.summary: The paper discusses CR7 as a z=6.6 luminous LAE and candidate Population III galaxy, then argues that its inferred stellar mass and luminosity exceed the model limits.

quotes.quote: Recently Sobral15 have reported the detection of He ii from a bright LAE at z=6.6 , which is called CR7. CR7 is very luminous in with L_ lya = 10^ 43.9   erg s^ 1 , has a high EW 200 , and no detection of metal lines (see however, Bowler et al. 2016 for metal line detections in CR7). In, addition, it is close to a system of star forming galaxies at a projected distance of 5  kpc . Based on SED modelling the authors suggest that CR7 could host a POPIII star cluster with M_ * 10^ 7   .
We show that the POPIII galaxy candidate CR7 violates the derived limits on stellar mass and luminosity and thus is unlikely to be a POPIII galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses CR7 as an ambiguous z=6.6 Lyman-break galaxy with strong Lyman-alpha and He II emission, possible Population III or direct-collapse black-hole interpretations, and unresolved modeling needs.

quotes.quote: A case in point is that of the z=6.6 Lyman break galaxy CR7. This source was selected for its extremely strong Lyman- line emission, but also shows strong He II 1640 \ emission, with constraints on the non-detection of O III 1665 \ and C III ] 1909 \ lines 2015ApJ...808..139S . Together with its photometric colours, these characteristics have been proposed as indicative of a metal-free (Population III) stellar population, while alternative interpretations include a primordial Direct Collapse Black Hole 2015MNRAS.453.2465P . Either would be somewhat surprising, given the source's luminosity and redshift. As 2016arXiv160900727B discuss, stellar evolution models can go some way towards addressing this question, but are unable to resolve the issue completely and the interpretation of CR7 will remain ambiguous until further observations and improved modelling of all possible scenarios are undertaken.

sources.summary: The paper presents ALMA follow-up of CR7, a z=6.6 luminous Lyman-alpha emitter, resolving multiple C II components and finding no FIR dust-continuum detection.

quotes.quote: Here we present deep spectroscopic observations of the COSMOS Redshift 7 galaxy (CR7) with ALMA, targeting the far-infrared dust continuum emission and the [ C ii ] line. CR7 has been identified as the most luminous Ly emitter (LAE) at z=6.6 based on narrow-band imaging with Subaru/Suprime-Cam Matthee2015 , with L _ Ly = 8.5 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 and EW _ 0, Ly = 210 Sobral2015 . Independently, identified CR7 as a candidate luminous Lyman-break galaxy at z 6-7 with ground-based near-infrared imaging. Hence, CR7 is also among the most UV-luminous galaxies known at z 7 , with M_ 1500 = -22.2 0.1 (see Matthee2017spec for even more luminous ones). Besides strong Ly emission, identified a narrow He ii emission-line in near-infrared spectroscopic follow-up observations, and obtained limits on UV metal lines such as C iv and C iii ] .

sources.summary: The paper discusses CR7 as a z=6.6 luminous Ly emitter proposed to host a 10^7 M_ Pop III stellar component, but argues its model cannot reproduce such a halo and notes ALMA evidence for a normal star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: Observations of the luminous Ly emitter CR7 by sobral_2015 have indicated the potential presence of a Pop III halo at z=6.6 with a stellar mass of 10^7 M_ . In order to find a halo with these properties in our model, we would have to break our single star-forming region assumption, as it is not possible for us to reach this mass with only a handful of massive Pop III stars. We would also have to allow stars to form massive Pop III stars in halos above the atomic cooling threshold, as it would otherwise be impossible for a halo to form such a high mass in stars (see Fig.  fig:min_masses_comp ). Recently, however, ALMA observations of this object have detected [ CII ] consistent with a normal star forming galaxy, so it is unlikely that CR7 actually contains 10^7 M _ in Pop III stars matthee_2017 .

sources.summary: The paper reviews CR7 as a z=6.6 galaxy whose Ly, HeII, metal-line, and photometric properties motivated debate over direct-collapse black hole, Population III, or faint-AGN interpretations.

quotes.quote: The detection of powerful Ly line emission, combined with the initial claim of a prominent HeII line (recently unconfirmed by the SILVERRUSH project shibuya:2017 ) and absence of detectable metal lines, in the z=6.6 galaxy CR7 raised hopes that a DCBH was finally identified. The peculiar emission properties of CR7 sobral:2015 could in principle arise from sources formed by pristine gas: either a cluster of Population III stars or a DCBH. Following , who compared the spectrum expected from a 10^5 M_ DCBH with the one of CR7, several authors agarwal:2016,hartwig:2016,visbal:2016,smith:2016,smidt:2016,dijkstra:2016 investigated the possible identification of CR7 with a DCBH. Although photometric observations by in the optical, near-IR and the mid-IR bands seem to favour a faint-AGN nature of CR7, confirm that those observations are still consistent with a DCBH and suggest that future spectroscopic observations with JWST are required to solve the puzzle.

sources.summary: The paper describes CR7 as a z=6.6 luminous Ly-alpha emitter with ALMA constraints on two regions, concluding its dust upper limit cannot distinguish SNe from AGB dust production.

quotes.quote: Cosmos Redshift 7 (CR7) located at z = 6.6 is the most luminous Ly emitter, discovered using the Subaru Telescope Matthee,Sobral2015 . From the ALMA [ CII ] line detection and dust continuum upper limit, estimated the dynamical masses and dust masses found in two regions of this galaxy, Clump A and Clump C-2 (Table Tab1 ).

HIMIKO, CR7, and SXDF only have upper limits for the mass of dust, so we cannot rule out either SNe or AGB stars as dust producers. We can only conclude that one SN in these galaxies could not produce more than 0.01 M_ of dust.

sources.summary: The paper describes CR7 as a z 6.6 Ly-alpha emitter that the authors previously suggested could host a DCBH, citing strong Ly-alpha/He II emission, absent metal lines, and uncertain X-ray confirmation.

quotes.quote: We proposed ( Pallottini_Pacucci_2015_CR7 , see also Agarwal_2015,Hartwig_2015, Visbal_2016, Smith_2016, Smidt_2016, Dijkstra_2016_CR7 ) that a z 6.6 object named CR7, the brightest Ly- emitter discovered so far Sobral_2015 , could be powered by a DCBH with an initial mass 10^5 M_ , associated with a standard stellar emission. This was motivated by the peculiarities of its spectrum, namely: (i) very strong Ly- and He II lines emission, and (ii) absence of metal lines, within the detection threshold. This proposition needs deeper X-ray observations to be confirmed or rejected.

sources.summary: The paper treats Cosmos Redshift 7 as an extremely luminous, apparently metal-poor Lyman-alpha source, noting direct-collapse black-hole interpretations and follow-up indications that it may be more metal enriched.

quotes.quote: Thus having an estimate for the Pop III metal enrichment will help to provide insight into the impact, frequency, origins and evolutionary path of extremely low metallicity systems such as Cosmos Redshift 7 (CR7), an extremely luminous Lyman- source that is seemingly devoid of metals Sobral:15 , and the Simcoe:12 DLA. Theoretical studies have suggested that a direct collapse black hole (DCBH) is a more likely explanation for the Sobral:15 observations e.g. Pallottini:15b,Smith:16,Agarwal:16,Agarwal:17,Pacucci:17 . Also, follow up observations of this system have also suggested that it may be more metal enriched than previously estimated Bowler:17,Matthee:17,Sobral:17 .

sources.summary: The review treats CR7 as a z=6.6 Lyman alpha emitter proposed to host a direct-collapse black hole, while noting Pop III seed growth remains equally plausible.

quotes.quote: There have been recent claims that direct collapse black holes have been observed, in particular for CR7, an estimated bright Lyman alpha emitter at z = 6.6 for which no metal lines could be detected (Sobral et al. 2016; Pacucci et al. 2016). While the presence of a massive black hole powering the emitter is a highly plausible hypothesis (Smith et al. 2016), the absence of metal lines in the spectrum, which would be consistent with the conventional notion that direct collapse black holes require metal-free gas, is not constraining the formation process, only the nature of the accreting gas. Therefore, a light seed black hole from Pop III stars, that has subsequently grown by Super-Eddington accretion in relatively pristine gas, is an equally plausible hypothesis.

gmw_00002054.name: NGC 247
gmw_00002054.overview: NGC 247 is an edge-on, low-surface-brightness spiral galaxy located in the nearby Sculptor Group [arXiv:2405.11749, arXiv:astro-ph/9911372]. The galaxy features an inactive nucleus [arXiv:1506.00790], ongoing star formation [arXiv:2405.11749], and a nuclear star cluster with a prolonged star formation history [arXiv:1807.08765].

sources.summary: The paper reports a deep XMM-Newton observation of NGC 247, describing its Sculptor Group distance, low star formation, ULX history, and an X-ray source population dominated by low-mass X-ray binaries.

quotes.quote: We report on a new XMM-Newton observation of NGC 247 from December 2009. The galaxy contains a supersoft, ultraluminous X-ray source (ULX) whose spectrum consists of a thermal component with a temperature about 0.1 keV and a power-law tail with a photon index around 2.5. The thermal emission is absolutely the dominant component, contributing 96 of the total luminosity in the 0.3-10 keV band.

NGC 247 is a nearby spiral galaxy in the Sculptor Group, with a modest star formation rate estimated to be about 0.1  M_  yr ^ 1 by counting bright main-sequence stars dav06 or based on H observations fer96 . However, infrared and radio observations suggest a star formation rate 10 or 100 times lower dav06 . The distance to NGC 247 has been measured independently by different groups using various means. Among them, the recent result from gie09 using near-infrared observations of Cepheids is claimed to be the most accurate. We therefore adopt that distance of 3.4 Mpc in this paper.

sources.summary: The paper reports a luminous nuclear X-ray flare in the nearby dormant bulgeless spiral NGC 247, discusses its inactive nucleus, and compares interpretations involving a stellar-mass or nuclear black hole.

quotes.quote: NGC 247 is a nearby late-type bulgeless spiral galaxy that contains an inactive nucleus. We report a serendipitous discovery of an X-ray flare from the galaxy center with a luminosity up to 2 10^ 39 \ in the 0.3--10 keV band with XMM-Newton . A Chandra observation confirms that the new X-ray source is spatially coincident with the galaxy nucleus. The XMM-Newton data revealed a hard power-law spectrum with a spectral break near 3--4 keV, no pulsations on timescales longer than 150 ms, and a flat power spectrum consistent with Poisson noise from 1 mHz to nearly 10 Hz. Follow-up observations with Swift detected a second flux peak followed by a luminosity drop by factor of almost 20.

NGC 247 contains a nuclear star cluster similar to other late-type spiral galaxies Davidge2002 . The dynamical center of the galaxy determined via 21 cm observations was found to be consistent with its optical center Carignan1990 . UV spectroscopy of the nuclear cluster Maoz1995 suggests that it is an \ region with no signature of an active galactic nucleus (AGN).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 247 as a star-forming edge-on SBd spiral with complex enrichment history, then confirms six associated star clusters spanning intermediate to old ages and broad metallicities.

quotes.quote: NGC 247 NGC 247 is an edge-on SBd spiral in the nearby Sculptor Group, with ongoing star formation activity and a stellar mass of 3 10^9 M_ . From a spectrum of the nucleus and using ppxf, Kacharov et al. (2018) determined its star formation history and found it to be very broad. At old ages (7--10 Gyr) it displays a wide range of metallicities (--2.3 < [ Fe/H ] < -0.5). This enriches to --1 < [ Fe/H ] < -0.5 around 2--3 Gyr ago. However, there are also some indications of star formation 10 Gyr ago that was metal-rich ( [ Fe/H ] 0), and 1 Gyr ago that was metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] -1.5). Thus it appears to have quite a complex star formation/chemical enrichment history.

Of the HST sources, we find all 6 to be bona fide star clusters associated with NGC 247. They have a range of ages and metallicities 4.3 < age < 10.6 Gyr --0.49 < [ Z/H ] < -2.0. Several of them might be better described as intermediate-aged star clusters.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 247 as the best-resolved LSB case, fitting its H I surface brightness and rotation curve to constrain a dark-matter-dominated halo.

quotes.quote: The LSB galaxy NGC 247 is a member of the nearby ( 2.5 Mpc) Sculptor group and has been observed in HI by Carignan Puche (1998). Surface photometry is taken from Carignan (1985b). The proximity of NGC 247 ensures that its HI velocity field has been very well resolved. The FWHM of the beam extends only 13 percent of the scalelength of the stellar disk, and the HI rotation curve is measured out to 25 beams. Therefore, this galaxy is the ideal candidate to obtain useful constraints on the density distribution of its dark matter halo.

The upper right panel shows the best fit model to the observed rotation curve. This model is completely dark matter dominated, and has a dark halo with = 1.02 , =7.2 , and V_ 200 = 93.1 . The best-fit mass-to-light ratio of the stellar disk is _B = 1.0 / , resulting in a baryon fraction of f_ bar = 0.011 .

sources.summary: NGC 247 is used with NGC 7793 to calibrate H-alpha photon-leakage corrections; its data support interpolating diffuse-ionized-gas contributions and leave the LIGIMF star-formation interpretation unchanged.

quotes.quote: The study of NGC 247 and NGC 7793 allows to construct a correction procedure in order to obtain the total H surface luminosities from the surface luminosities of H  ii regions only (see Supplementary Discussion). For a linear star-formation law ( N=1 ) as derived from UV observations the LIGIMF-theory predicts an H surface luminosity as a function of the gas surface density which is in full agreement with the observations (Fig.  kennicutt_schmidt_fig ). Additionally, the radial H profile derived in the LIGIMF-theory matches the observations perfectly (Fig.  kennicutt_radial_fig ).

This study of the two galaxies NGC 247 and NGC 7793 shows hat the radial and azimuthal surface luminosity of the DIG is highly correlated with bright H  ii regions and that the required power to sustain the DIG can only be met by the ionising radiation from massive star formation. In faint H luminosity regions the luminosity contribution of the DIG can exceed the one by distinct H  ii regions. We use the published values of the H surface luminosities of H  ii regions only and H  ii regions plus DIG in different regions of these galaxies to construct a correction formula.
With y = _ 10 _ HII _ HII + DIG and x = _ 10 _ HII the high luminosity region of NGC 7793 and all data points of NGC 247 can be easily interpolated (solid line) by y(x) = 0.22 (x-32.3) .

sources.summary: The paper reports deep H observations of NGC 247, detecting diffuse emission across the H I disk but not beyond it, deriving extended rotation curves, and finding no warped disk.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: In the World Coordinate System, we find that the dynamical centre is located at =00:54:50.4 =-37:39:48.0 for NGC 300, and at =00:47:08.6 =-20:45:27.3 for NGC 247.

quotes.quote: For NGC 247, emission extending up to a radius comparable with that of the H i disc ( 13' ) is found, but no emission is seen beyond the H i disc. For NGC 300, we detect ionized gas on the entirety of our field of view ( r_ max 14' ), and find that the bright H ii regions are embedded in a diffuse background. Using the deep data, extended optical rotation curves are obtained, as well as mass models. These are the most extended optical rotation curves thus far for these galaxies. We find no evidence suggesting that NGC 247 has a warped disc, and to account for our non detection of H emission beyond its H i disc, as opposed to the warped galaxy NGC 253, our results favour the model in which only through a warp, ionization by hot young stars in the central region of a galaxy can let photons escape and ionize the interstellar medium in the outer parts.

In the World Coordinate System, we find that the dynamical centre is located at =00:54:50.4 =-37:39:48.0 for NGC 300, and at =00:47:08.6 =-20:45:27.3 for NGC 247. Next, we determine i and PA, while the dynamical centre and V_ sys are kept fixed at the extracted values. Separate solutions are obtained for the receding and approaching side ( i. e. using data points only on the receding or approaching side of the galaxy).

sources.summary: The paper uses LVL mosaics for NGC 247 and notes that its inclination and small image coverage make contamination significant, with CMDs showing two source populations.

quotes.quote: We present Spitzer IRAC 3.6-8 and MIPS 24 point-source catalogs for seven galaxies: NGC 6822 , M 33 , NGC 300 , NGC 2403 , M 81 , NGC 0247 , and NGC 7793 . The catalogs contain a total of 300,000 sources and were created by dual-band selection of sources with >3 detections at both 3.6 and 4.5 . The source lists become significantly incomplete near m_ 3.6 =m_ 4.5 18 . We complement the 3.6 and 4.5 fluxes with 5.8 , 8.0 and 24 fluxes or 3 upper limits using a combination of PSF and aperture photometry.
For NGC 300 and NGC 247, we used the mosaics from the LVL survey ref:Dale_2009 .

The contamination is significant for galaxies like NGC 247, which is highly inclined and covers a smaller fraction of the Spitzer images, than for the larger and more face-on galaxies (see Figure fig:galaxies ). Indeed, the CMDs of NGC 247 (Figure fig:cmd16b , second column) clearly show two distinct population of sources, with the break appearing near m_ 4.5 18 .

sources.summary: The paper applies a thick-disc model to NGC 247's SUL-to-SSUL X-ray transitions, attributing hard-band dips and spectral changes to a puffed-up inner disc that shields and reprocesses emission.

quotes.quote: We propose the thick-disc model of Gu2016 to interpret the transition between soft ultraluminous state (SUL) and supersoft ultraluminous (SSUL) state in NGC 247. As accretion rate increases, the inner disc will puff up and act as shield to block the innermost X-ray emission regions and absorb both soft and hard X-ray photons. The absorbed X-ray emission will be re-radiated as a much softer blackbody X-ray spectrum. Hence NGC 247 shows flux dips in the hard X-ray band and transits from the SUL state to the SSUL state.

Indeed, by analyzing data of XMM-Newton , Chandra , Swift and Hubble Space Telescope observations, Feng2016 first found the transitions between the supersoft ultraluminous (SSUL) regime and the soft ultraluminous regime (SUL) in the spectra of NGC 247. In the SSUL regime, the X-ray spectrum of NGC 247 can be well characterized by a cool (i.e., the temperature T 0.1 keV) blackbody component which is similar to other ULSs. For the first time, the X-ray spectrum of NGC 247 in the SUL regime shows a strong power-law component with flux comparable to the soft blackbody emission.

sources.summary: The VLA-ANGST paper highlights NGC 247 as a bright, wide-line H I target with 4IF observations, full-bandwidth coverage, RFI interpolation, and the maximum linewidth in the sample.

quotes.quote: NGC 247 was also observed in the 4IF mode because of its extreme \ linewidth of 200 , by far the widest line of our sample.

For the two galaxies that were observed in the 4IF mode, KK 77 and NGC 247, the full bandwidths were required to cover the requested velocity ranges. The aliased signal appears in each IF and, consequently, the full noise variations of up to 35 are visible across the combined spectrum. Overall, however, this will not have a huge impact on most of the data analysis; KK 77 is a non-detection, and NGC 247 is one of our brightest objects such that the signal-to-noise ratio is only moderately affected.

In the case of NGC 247, velocities around 108 were strongly contaminated with RFI; to estimate the flux in this channel, we interpolated the emission from the adjacent channels. Since our sample is composed primarily of dwarf galaxies, the galaxy spectra typically show narrow, singly-peaked profiles. Extreme cases like KDG 73 and KKH 86 exhibit linewidths of <10 , which implies very little velocity dispersion, maybe due to low signal to noise in the line wings. On the other end of the mass spectrum, a few galaxies (NGC 404 NGC 3741, DDO 190, Sextans A, DDO 181, NGC 3109, NGC 247) exhibit hints of the more familiar double-horned profile expected from larger spiral disks. The maximum linewidth is observed in NGC 247 with w_ 20 200 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 247 as a bulgeless dwarf spiral whose nuclear star cluster formed stars over a prolonged history, with recent super-solar and very young components.

quotes.quote: NGC 247 is a bulgeless dwarf spiral galaxy (SAB(s)d) at a distance 3.6 Mpc (the quoted distances in this work are the median estimates from NED https://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ ) and a member of the Sculptor group. carson+2015 report a colour gradient in its NSC - the effective radius is smallest in UV and largest in IR, which suggests the presence of multiple stellar populations, and that the young stars are centrally concentrated and likely formed in situ .

NGC 247's NSC seems to have experienced a very prolonged star formation that continues until the present day. It built most of its mass in the early Universe ( 50 already present 10 Gyr ago) at a relatively low metallicity ( 1.5 dex but with a significant spread), with younger generations being more and more metal rich on average, reaching 0.5 dex roughly 1 Gyr ago and 90 of the present day mass. A significant increase of the SFR has occurred 100-500 Myr ago giving birth to a population of super-Solar metallicity and contributing 10 of the total mass. There is also an indication of a very young component ( 15 Myr but contributing a negligible fraction of the total mass <1 ), which is further supported by the presence of strong emission lines in the nucleus and the surrounding region, indicative of ongoing star formation.

sources.summary: The paper reports VRI Cepheid observations in the Sculptor Group spiral NGC 0247 and derives a Cepheid distance modulus consistent with recent Cepheid and TRGB distances.

quotes.quote: We report VRI CCD observations of nine Cepheids in the South Polar (Sculptor) Group spiral galaxy NGC 0247. Periods of these Cepheids range from 20 to 70 days.

A multi-wavelength analysis of the Cepheid data yields a true distance modulus of _o = 27.81 0.10 mag (3.36 0.16 Mpc) with a total line-of-sight reddening of E(V-I) = 0.07 0.04 mag, after adopting an LMC true distance modulus of 18.5 mag and reddening of E(B-V) = 0.10 mag. These results are in excellent agreement with other very recently published (Cepheid and TRGB) distances to NGC 0247. Combining both Cepheid datasets gives _o = 27.85 0.09 mag (3.72 0.15 Mpc) with E(V-I) = 0.11 0.03 mag.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 247 as a Sculptor Group galaxy and finds its nuclear cluster has a UV-to-IR color gradient, increasing effective radius, and axis-ratio changes suggesting young stars in a disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 247 is a member of the Sculptor Group, at a distance 3.4 Mpc bono2010 . We fit a flat sky level to the host galaxy and a single \ component to the NC in all bands. We find evidence for a color gradient within this cluster as well. The effective radius is smallest in the UV and increases towards the IR, with a best-fit value of 0.79^ +0.07 _ 0.07 in F275W and 3.49^ +1.80 _ 0.69 in F153M. The best-fit axis ratio is 0.55^ +0.01 _ 0.01 in F275W and monotonically increases to 0.89^ +0.01 _ 0.01 in F153M, which suggests that the young stars formed in a disk.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 247 as an extreme extended FIR source whose Scanpi flux exceeds catalog values, requiring zero-point flux density and careful background fitting.

quotes.quote: We also notice two clear outliers, NGC 247 and NGC 300. They have much larger Scanpi flux densities at the same catalogue flux density level than other objects. In the left panel of Figure fig:scanpi , we show the scan data of NGC 247, an extreme example of extended source. In such case, we use zero-point flux density. But it is necessary to manually adjust the background fitting region (especially the exclusion region) to make sure the background is not overestimated.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 247 as a nearby southern dwarf spiral with M33-like star formation and metallicity, comparable H luminosity to M31/M33/LMC, and two studied LBV candidates.

quotes.quote: So, our survey of Local Volume galaxies which aim to discover of LBVs, firstly presented in the paper Solovyeva19 , is continuing with study of NGC 247. The dwarf spiral galaxy (SAB(s)d) NGC 247 is one of the closest spiral galaxies of the southern sky. The star formation rate and metallicity of this galaxy is similar to those of M33 galaxy, but NGC 247 is more inclined towards the line of sight. The H luminosity of NGC 247 is comparable to those ones of M31, M33 and LMC Kennicutt2008 . In this paper we present the discovery and detailed investigation of two new LBV candidates in NGC 247: j004703.27-204708.4 and j004702.18-204739.93 (hereafter \ and , respectively).

sources.summary: The paper reports SkyMapper photometry for NGC 247 and explains that its diffuse dwarf structure prevented convergence of fitted ellipses, leading the authors to assign a larger photometric error.

quotes.quote: SkyMapper Photometry Photometry was obtained for four galaxies from the calibration sample (NGC 55, NGC 247, NGC 253, and NGC 7793) from observations performed using the ANU SkyMapper 1.35m telescope K07 , located at Siding Spring Observatory, on September 23 and 24, 2010.

The difficulties encountered are particularly evident for NGC 247, whose integrated magnitude curve exhibits no convergence for the fitted ellipses. We can attribute this result to the diffuse nature of this dwarf galaxy, where the fitting algorithms struggled to define an outer luminous boundary. For this particular object, we assign a larger error of 0.1 mag.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 16 M-type RSGs and a bright YSG in NGC 247, and uses line ratios and RSG temperatures to discuss its metallicity.

quotes.quote: NGC 247 We identified 16 M-type RSGs in NGC 247.
NGC 247 hosts a bright YSG (NGC247-1786).

The case of NGC 247 (0.4 ) is noteworthy, as the line ratios overlap with those of NGC 55 (0.27 ) and NGC 1313 (0.35 ) especially for three \ regions located in the outskirts of the galaxy, instead of those of NGC 300 and NGC 7793 ( 0.4 ), implying a metallicity of 0.3 for this galaxy. However, the identification of 16 M-type RSG a narrow effective temperature range of RSG was also noted by Davidge2021 in this galaxy is consistent with a metallicity of 0.4 .

sources.summary: NGC 247 is described as a nearby low-surface-brightness spiral whose H I includes a filamentary CNM network, loss of CNM beyond R25, and about 70 percent of flux in CNM.

quotes.quote: Several low mass spirals were observed in the high resolution sample of Braun ( , ), including the nearby SBm galaxy NGC 2366 and two of the nearest low surface brightness spirals, NGC 247 (of type Sc) and NGC 4236 (SBd). A filamentary network of CNM gas is seen in all these systems, which accounts for about 80 of the line flux from only about 15 of the surface area within R_ 25 (the radius where the average face-on surface brightness of light in the B band has declined to 25 mag arsec ^ 2 ). Just beyond R_ 25 the flux fraction of CNM gas disappears entirely, even though the WNM continues out to a radius of almost twice R_ 25 . The result is that the total \ flux fraction due to CNM in these galaxies is about 70 , substantially more than the 40 CNM fraction in the CHVCs.

gmw_00002055.name: NGC 5102
gmw_00002055.overview: NGC 5102 is a nucleated dwarf lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1807.08765] located in the M 83 group [arXiv:1807.08765] that is classified as a post-starburst galaxy [arXiv:1012.2807]. It features counter-rotating stellar discs [arXiv:1610.04516], a relatively large H I gas content [arXiv:1610.04516], and a young, blue nuclear stellar population [arXiv:1807.08765, arXiv:1610.04516].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5102 as a nucleated dwarf lenticular with a young, high-metallicity nuclear population, possible post-starburst character, and central SMBH evidence.

quotes.quote: NGC 5102 is a nucleated dwarf lenticular (S0) galaxy in the M 83 group of galaxies at a distance of 3.7 Mpc. Previous studies based on resolved stars and spectral synthesis have shown that the galaxy is dominated by an intermediate age ( 0.1 - 3 Gyr) stellar population towards its centre kraft+2005,davidge2008,davidge2015,mitzkus+2017 . mitzkus+2017 find a mass weighted age of 0.8 Gyr and their composite population fit favours the existence of a young population of 0.3 Gyr at solar metallicity together with an old ( >10 Gyr), metal poor ( <-1 ) population.

This is the only NSC, in which we do not find an evidence for a population older than 10 Gyr and the most significant star formation happened less than 1 Gyr ago. We find a mass weighted age of 1.4 Gyr and light weighted age of 0.7 Gyr, while mitzkus+2017 estimate a mass weighted age of 0.8 Gyr. Comparing it to our SSP resolution (Figure fig:all_SSP_max_regul_light ), it is consistent with being a SSP. The best fit SSP solution also has the same ^2 value as the composite fit (Table tab:age_feh_ssp ). However, an older population may simply be completely outshined by a massive young population, making NGC 5102 a post-starburst galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5102 as a nearby lenticular galaxy with very few luminous X-ray binaries, young stellar populations, few globular clusters, and central diffuse hot gas.

quotes.quote: We present results from a 34 ks Chandra/ACIS-S observation of the LMXB population and the hot ISM in the nearby (d=3.1 Mpc) lenticular galaxy NGC 5102, previously shown to have an unusually low X-ray luminosity. We detect eleven X-ray point sources within the the D_ 25 optical boundary of the galaxy (93 of the light), one third to one half of which are likely to be background AGN. One of the X-ray sources is coincident with the optical nucleus and may be a low-luminosity AGN. Only two sources with an X-ray luminosity greater than 10 ^ 37 ergs s ^ 1 in the 0.5-5.0 keV band were detected, one of which is statistically likely to be a background AGN. We expected to detect 7 or 5 such luminous sources if the XRB population scales linearly with the B band or J band magnitudes, respectively, of the host galaxy.

By this measure, NGC 5102 has an unusually low number of XRBs. The deficit of LMXBs is even more striking because some of these sources may in fact be HMXBs. NGC 5102 is unusually blue for its morphological type, and has undergone at least two recent bursts of star formation only 1.5 10 ^7 and 3 10 ^8 years ago.

sources.summary: MUSE observations reveal counter-rotating discs in NGC 5102, whose S0 structure, blue nucleus, large H I content, dark halo, and IMF-halo degeneracy make it unusual among modeled early-type galaxies.

quotes.quote: The kinematic maps reveal for the first time that NGC 5102 has the characteristic 2 peaks indicative of galaxies with counter-rotating discs. This interpretation is quantitatively confirmed by fitting two kinematic components to the observed spectra.

The photometry of NGC 5102 shows the typical features of an S0 galaxy: a bulge that follows a ^ 1/4 profile and a disc with a flatter profile at larger radii 1979ApJ...231..354P, 2015ApJ...799...97D . The central deviation from the ^ 1/4 profile builds the nucleus. Other observations show that NGC 5102 is an unusual S0 galaxy: The nucleus has an unusually blue colour first mentioned by Freeman in 1971QJRAS..12..305E for S0 galaxies and is interpreted as a star cluster with an age of 10^8 yr e.g. 1975ApJ...202....7G, 1976AJ.....81..795V, 1979ApJ...231..354P . The second peculiarity about NGC 5102 is the relatively large H I content of 3.3 10^8 M _ 1975ApJ...202....7G .

Including an NFW dark matter halo, we need a heavy IMF and a dark matter fraction of 0.37 0.04 within a sphere of one R_ e radius to describe the stellar kinematics. The more general model with a free slope of the dark matter halo shows that slope and IMF are degenerate, but indicates that a light weight IMF (Chabrier-like) and a higher dark matter fraction, with a steeper (contracted) halo, fit the data better.

sources.summary: The paper builds a two-NSC model for S0 galaxy NGC 5102, tracks its stability, and finds IMBH coalescence can occur efficiently, especially for retrograde eccentric binaries.

quotes.quote: NGC 5102 NGC 5102 exhibits the morphological attributes of an S0 type galaxy dav08 . Kinematic measurements yield a rotational velocity of 20 km/s and a velocity dispersion in range of 44-60 km/s, with higher values observed near the center  mit17 . v/ is around 0.4 close to the center and increases to 0.7 at larger radii ngu18 . The kinematic profile suggests that the inner NSC is weakly rotating while the outer NSC is a flattened ( a_2/a_1 0.6 ) and strongly rotating system, so we choose to model these components as two separate NSCs.

In the case of NGC 5102, the relatively mild central rotation is not enough to flip the binary's plane, so the binary retains its retrograde orientation and grows highly eccentric. This high eccentricity ensures that the IMBHs merge very efficiently on nearly radial orbits in about 23.42 Myr. In fact, despite the inevitable circularization of the binary orbit once gravitational radiation is strong, we estimate that the binary can enter the LISA regime with a detectable eccentricity of order 0.01  peters+63 .

sources.summary: The paper resolves NGC 5102’s nuclear star cluster, finding young metal-rich central populations, older metal-poor outer populations, and links to distinct structural components.

quotes.quote: Utilizing full-spectrum fitting, we find substantial age and metallicity gradients within the central 0 9 (16 pc) of the NSC in NGC 5102 where populations near the center are young/metal-rich (age 400 Myr and [ M/H ] 0.4) and become older/metal-poor at larger radii (mean age 1 Gyr and mean [ M/H ] 1.6 in the radial range [ 0 3, 0 9 ] ). This behavior suggests that the young/metal-rich population at the center was formed from a period of in situ formation, while the older/metal-poor populations were likely formed by inspiraled globular clusters. The two broad populations observed in the NGC 5102 NSC (young/metal-rich and old/metal-poor) appear to be linked to the transition between the two morphological components of the NSC derived from the surface-brightness profile in Nguyen2018 .

The presence of a young (LW age 400 Myr) and relatively metal-rich (LW [ M/H ] 0.4) population at the very center suggests a period of in situ star formation in this region. We note that this population could result from the accretion of young star clusters that formed near the galaxy center. However, the high central concentration of this population (shown in Figure fig:bestparams ) further argues for in situ formation. The older (LW age 1 Gyr) and metal-poor (LW [ M/H ] 1.6) populations present at radii between 0 3 and 0 9 are consistent with the cluster inspiral formation mechanism. This metallicity is much lower than both the very central regions of the NSC in our data, and the dominant population for the central regions of the galaxy from (Figure fig:maps ), which has an average [ M/H ] of roughly -0.2 (but with some lower metallicity spaxels at similar radii to where we find low metallicities). These low metallicities suggest that these populations likely formed in GCs at larger radii that migrated into the NSC.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5102 as a blue S0 post-starburst galaxy with recent nuclear star-formation shutdown, using NIR indices to recover its young stellar population.

quotes.quote: Likewise, the blue S0 galaxy NGC 5102 is close and bright enough for high SNR integrated spectroscopy plus resolved CMD analysis of its brightest stars. It is classified as a post-starburst (PSB) galaxy because its strong Balmer-line absorption and absence of emission indicates that star formation terminated recently. Studies by, e.g., deharveng97,davidge08 have indicated that the nuclear region of NGC 5102 has undergone a termination of star formation within the past 10--100 Myr, after a several hundred Myr period of activity, guaranteeing that there is an appreciable young population with a mean age of a few 100 Myr.

The intermediate age stars in M32 and the younger stars in NGC 5102 are a useful pairing because most of the NIR light emitted by these two galaxies comes from stars in different evolutionary stages: the younger population will be dominated by the TP-AGB and a blue MSTO, while in the intermediate age population most of the light comes from the red giant branch and an older MSTO (see Fig. 13 in M05).

sources.summary: The paper flags NGC 5102 as a massive-galaxy outlier whose sfind radio flux may miss extended emission, while aperture photometry has larger flux but lower significance.

quotes.quote: This specific object, NGC 5102, is catalogued with the sfind flux density, which may miss the extended emission, causing a unreal small radio flux density. Its aperture flux density is indeed about 3 times larger. Unfortunately, the optical aperture for this object is too large, probably including too many empty pixels, causing an overestimation of flux uncertainty. The signal-to-noise ratio of its aperture flux density is slightly smaller than 3, failed to pass the detection threshold. We note, however, it is not the typical situation for our galaxies (see Figure fig:cont_1 ).

sources.summary: The paper flags NGC 5102 as an anomalous post-starburst E-S0 galaxy with exceptionally low X-ray luminosity, likely affected by gas removal and a young blue stellar population boosting its optical light.

quotes.quote: NGC 5102 is a relatively small E--S0 galaxy (Log L _B = 9.29 L _ ) with an exceptionally low X--ray luminosity (Log L _X = 38.03 erg s ^ 1 ). It is thought to have undergone an episode of star formation a few 10 ^8 years ago ( BicaAlloin87 ). Although during and shortly after the starburst we might expect to observe an enhanced L _X compared to L _B ( Readponman98 ), galactic wind models predict that the starburst can remove all gas from the galaxy, leaving it significantly underluminous until the halo is rebuilt ( ciotti91 ; pellegriniciotti98 ). The B--band luminosity will also be enhanced by the population of young stars produced by the starburst, making the position of such an object on an L _X :L _B plot even more aberrant.

As mentioned in Section Res2 , NGC 5102 is a recent ( 400 Myr) post--starburst galaxy and has a population of young blue stars ( BicaAlloin87 ) which may have `artificially' raised its B band luminosity.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5102 as a nearby early-type galaxy with direct massive-star-formation evidence, detailed UV study, and very blue UV-V color benchmarking residual star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 205 and NGC 5102 are well known examples of nearby early-type galaxies with direct evidence for massive star formation (Hodge , Pritchet ). NGC 205 and NGC 5102 have also been studied in details in the UV domain (Bertola et al. , Jones et al. , Cappellari et al. , Deharveng et al. ). The relative contribution of the different families of low-mass stars in advanced stages of evolution For the moment, we concentrate on the spread in UV-V color, irrespectively of the scatter in B-V , and defer discussion of the bluest objects ( UV-V < 1.5) close to NGC 205 ( UV-V = 1.19) and NGC 5102 ( UV-V = 0.8) to the following subsection. Our sample contains seven objects with UV color bluer than 1.4, comparable to those of NGC 205 ( UV-V = 1.19) and NGC 5102 ( UV-V = 0.80).

gmw_00002056.name: spiral host galaxy of GRB 060505
gmw_00002056.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 060505 is a face-on, Sc-type spiral galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.089 [arXiv:1404.0881, arXiv:astro-ph/0703192]. It exhibits regular rotation without large perturbations [arXiv:1404.0881] and has an overall star formation rate of about 2 solar masses per year [arXiv:astro-ph/0703192].

sources.summary: The paper maps the GRB 060505 host, finding a low-metallicity GRB site, normal host HII-region properties, radial gradients, regular rotation, and no clear major perturbation.

quotes.quote: The spiral host galaxy of GRB 060505 at z=0.089 was the site of a puzzling long duration burst without an accompanying supernova. Studies of the burst environment by Th one et al. (2008) suggested that this GRB came from the collapse of a massive star and that the GRB site was a region with properties different from the rest of the galaxy. We reobserved the galaxy in high spatial resolution using the VIMOS integral-field unit (IFU) at the VLT with a spaxel size of 0.67 arcsec.

We compare the properties of different HII regions in the galaxy with the GRB site and study the global and local kinematic properties of this galaxy. The resolved data show that the GRB site has the lowest metallicity in the galaxy with 1/3 Z _ , but its specific SFR (SSFR) of 7.4 M _ /yr/L/L* and age (determined by the H EW) are similar to other HII regions in the host. The galaxy shows a gradient in metallicity and SSFR from the bulge to the outskirts as it is common for spiral galaxies. This gives further support to the theory that GRBs prefer regions of higher star-formation and lower metallicity, which, in S-type galaxies, are more easily found in the spiral arms than in the centre. Kinematic measurements of the galaxy do not show evidence for large perturbations but a minor merger in the past cannot be excluded.

sources.summary: The paper describes 2dFGRS S173Z112 as a face-on Sc galaxy hosting GRB 060505, with a nearby bright star-forming knot, measurable star formation, and optical magnitudes.

quotes.quote: As seen in Fig. HST_Image , GRB 060505 is spatially associated with a bright, spectroscopically confirmed, star forming region found at a projected distance of 7.1 kpc ( 4 3 ) from its host galaxy center. The AB magnitude of this star forming knot is 23.9 and 23.7 in the F475W and F814W-bands, respectively. Our observations (Fig. HST_Image and HostGalSpec_P200red ) show that 2dFGRS S173Z112 is a face-on Sc-type galaxy. Using the flux of the H emission line, along with the relation from Kennicutt (1998), we estimate that the star formation rate in this galaxy is about 2 M _ yr ^ 1 .

We obtained this value by extrapolating the H emission, observed within the slit, over the entire area of the galaxy, weighted by the g-band surface brightness. We note that the total AB magnitude of the galaxy is 18.3 and 17.7 in the F475W and F814W-bands, respectively. This is equivalent to absolute magnitude of 19.4 and 20.4 in the F475W and F814W-bands, respectively (corrected for K-correction; Oke Sandage 1968).

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 060505 host as a spiral whose burst lies in a young, highly star-forming, relatively metal-poor arm region, unlike the older solar-metallicity host as a whole.

quotes.quote: Both the host of GRB 060505, one of the SN-less GRBs, and the host of GRB 980425 / SN 1998bw were spiral galaxies with the GRB found inside a star-forming region in a spiral arm.

GRB 060505 - a SN-less GRB We took a longslit spectrum of this host with FORS2/VLT across the galaxy, divided into 5 regions Thoene08a . HST images pinpoint the GRB to a large SR region in a spiral arm and the FORS2 analysis showed it to be a young, highly starforming region with relatively low metallicity whereas the rest of the host and has a much older stellar population and solar metallicity. This makes the burst very likely to be connected to the collapse of a massive star.
A detailed mapping of the galaxy is planned with an approved proposal for VIMOS/IFU.

sources.summary: The paper describes the LGRB 060505 host as a well-resolved spiral at z 0.089, with the burst on an HII region used to justify long-burst inclusion.

quotes.quote: The host LGRB 060505 (z 0.089) is a well resolved spiral 060505 . The LGRB occurred right on top of an HII region. No supernova was seen and strong limits have been placed on the magnitude of the underlying supernova Ofek . There has been some discussion as to whether this burst was actually a long or a short burst Ofek, 060505 . However, due to the location of the burst on an HII region, and the bursts 4s duration, we have included it in our long burst sample.

gmw_00002057.name: OI 090.4
gmw_00002057.overview: OI 090.4 is a highly polarized, variable low-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1807.02926, arXiv:1605.03599] located at a redshift of z = 0.266 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306617]. The active galaxy features a faint resolved host [arXiv:1605.03599], a companion galaxy at a projected distance of 70 kpc [arXiv:astro-ph/0306617], and a diffuse radio halo [arXiv:1006.4777].

sources.summary: The paper identifies OI 090.4 as a BL Lac/LBL with uncertain early redshift estimates and monitored stronger bluer-when-brighter color behavior.

quotes.quote: OI 090.4 was first identified as a BL Lac object by 1977ApJ...215L..71T . Cross identification of optical and radio source was accomplished by 1977AJ.....82..692C . A tentative redshift z = 0.66 was proposed by 1986ASSL..121..675P and was then questioned by 1991MNRAS.252..482A .

The amplitude of the variations is systematically larger at higher frequencies (see Table observations ). As can be seen in Figure fig:all , the average spectral index was 1.46. One can see from Figure fig:allcolor , OI 090.4 shows a stronger BWB trend with i-o color than with the i and o colors.

sources.summary: The paper confirms PKS 0754+100 as a polarized variable blazar/BL Lac object, measures z=0.266 from emission lines, and derives a host-galaxy luminosity.

quotes.quote: PKS 0754+100 is a highly polarized and variable source that was discovered by Tapia et al. (1977) and classified as a blazar by Angel Stockman (1980). A first tentative redshift proposed by Persic and Salucci (1986, z=0.66) was questioned after the detection of the host galaxy by Abraham et al. (1991) and by Falomo (1996), who suggested z 0.3 on the basis of the host galaxy properties (at z=0.66 the host would be extremely luminous: M _ R 25).

Our spectrum (see figure 1) shows clearly two emission lines at =4717.85 and =6339 (see Table 2): identification of these features with [ OII ] (3727 ) and [ OIII ] (5007 ) yields a redshift <z> =0.266 0.001. This is consistent with the preliminary redshift proposed by Falomo and Ulrich (2000, z 0.28). At z=0.266 the host galaxy luminosity becomes M _ R =-22.9. We note that at the same redshift there is a companion galaxy, 13 '' NE, corresponding to a projected distance of 70 kpc (Pesce et al. 1995).

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0754 + 100 as a highly polarized variable LSP BL Lac, with long-term optical variability, infrared and radio variability, a faint resolved host, and a low activity state.

quotes.quote: PKS 0754 + 100: is a highly polarized and variable LSP blazar Ghosh95,Ghosh00 of the BL-Lac type Tapia77 . Its long-term variability at optical bands can be traced back to 1980 e.g. Baumert80,Pica88,Sillanpaa91,Katajainen00 . Infrared variations were detected by FanLin99 , as well as a radio flare by Nieppola09 . This object shows a featureless spectrum and its host galaxy can be resolved in the images Abraham91,Falomo96 , but it is rather faint compared to the AGN. Nilsson03 suggest a host magnitude of R=18.55 mag and r_ eff =1.3 arcsec.

sources.summary: The paper classifies B0754 + 100 as a low-synchrotron-peaked BL Lac/blazar with a large diffuse radio halo and recent near-infrared flaring.

quotes.quote: B0754 + 100 B0754 + 100 is cataloged as a low-frequency peaked BL Lac (LBL) by fiorucci04 . By the blazar classification of abdo10b , B0754 + 100 is a low synchrotron peaked blazar ( _ peak 10^ 14 Hz) based on our fitting result. VLA observations showed that there is a diffuse halo around the core at 1.5 and 5 GHz up to scales of > 120 kpc antonucci85, kollgaard92 . Recently, B0754 + 100 was reported to be flaring in the near-infrared band carrasco10 .

gmw_00002058.name: 2E3140
gmw_00002058.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2E3140 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy located within the galaxy cluster A1795 [arXiv:1907.07642, arXiv:2108.04827]. Also classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, its X-ray emission is reflection-dominated with a hidden illuminating continuum originating from a very compact region [arXiv:0810.2272].

sources.summary: The paper uses the Seyfert galaxy 2E3140 in A1795 to test ALP spectral classifiers, finding consistent machine-learning bounds that improve the previous limit for this source.

quotes.quote: Figure spectra shows the observed spectrum of the Seyfert galaxy 2E3140 in the galaxy cluster A1795, and its simulated spectrum assuming the existence of ALPs with g_ a = 5 10^ 12 GeV ^ 1 and a particular realisation of the A1795 magnetic field. We see that ALPs induce characteristic oscillations in the residuals, with larger wavelengths than those from Poisson fluctuations alone.

Figures GaussianNBResid and GaussianNBUp show the performance of our classifiers C _g when queried with data simulated with different ALP-photon couplings g_ query for the Sy1 galaxy 2E3140 within A1795. We see that for classifiers trained with sufficiently high g values, data simulated with ALPs is mostly classified as such, while data simulated without ALPs is also mostly classified correctly. For all three data processing methods, we see a clear separation between ALP and no ALP data.

On the other hand, applying machine learning classifiers to the Seyfert 1 galaxy within A1795 consistently gives bounds in the range g_ a 0.7 - 1.2 10^ 12 GeV ^ 1 . This is in a similar range to the current leading bound obtained using a significantly higher resolution data set with conventional statistical methods. Furthermore, it improves the previously reported bound in of g_ a 1.5 10^ 12 GeV ^ 1 for this source.

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM-Newton spectra of EXO 1346.2+2645, finding a reflection-dominated NLS1 with hidden continuum emission, compact inner-disk source region, and constant RMS/hardness behavior.

quotes.quote: Multi-epoch XMM-Newton spectra of the two Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1), Mrk 478 and EXO 1346.2+2645, are presented. The data were fitted with different models, including the relativistically-blurred reflection model reflionx , which was found to give a good description of both spectra in all epochs. The two sources are reflection dominated with the illuminating continuum hidden from view. This can be explained either in terms of a corrugated disc or strong gravitational light bending effects.

The emission from these two sources comes from a very small region (few gravitational radii), very close to a rapidly spinning black hole. Spectral variability analysis show that both sources have constant RMS spectra, and constant hardness ratios in all epochs, in agreement with a single spectral component. The constancy the spectrum between high and low states rules out alternative models such as partial covering.

sources.summary: The paper uses the Sy1 galaxy 2E3140 in A1795 as a Chandra point source, showing photon-survival, fitted-spectrum and fake-spectrum examples and deriving an upscaled 1D ALP constraint for it.

quotes.quote: An illustrative example of a photon survival probability is given in Figure fig:surv_prob for one of the sources we discuss in this article, the type I Seyfert galaxy 2E3140 within A1795.
For instance, the fit of the spectrum of the Sy1 2E3140 galaxy within A1795 is shown on the left of Figure fig:sherpa_comparison .

In order to check whether the better bounds arise from the differences in the strength of the magnetic field, we derive the constraint using the upscaled 1D model presented in Section sec:mag_fields for the source A1795Sy1. For that we obtain g_ a 1.1 10^ 12 GeV ^ 1 which is already closer to the constraint from the 3D model but still not as tight.

gmw_00002059.name: B1256+281
gmw_00002059.overview: B1256+281 is a quasar located behind the Coma galaxy cluster [arXiv:1907.07642, arXiv:2108.04827]. It is a hard X-ray source [arXiv:0705.2722] with a spectrum that is well fitted by a simple power-law model [arXiv:0705.2722, arXiv:2108.04827].

sources.summary: The paper tests the quasar EXO 1256+281 as a possible hard X-ray point source near the Coma SW excess, extracting its spectrum but finding it unlikely to explain the extension.

quotes.quote: If the SW excess is due to the presence of a point source with a hard spectrum, we show that there is no obvious X-ray counterpart for this additional source, and that the closest X-ray source is the quasar EXO 1256+281, which is located 6.1' from the centroid of the excess.

Although there is strong evidence that EXO 1256+281 cannot explain the \ SW extension, the angular distance between the fitted position and EXO 1256+281 (6.1') is close to the half-width of the ISGRI PSF. Hence, we further investigate this point by extracting the spectral properties of this object, assuming the identification of the additional point source with EXO 1256+281. To this end, we fitted the excess in the ISGRI image by a point source at the position of EXO 1256+281, and extracted the flux for this source. Fig. exospec shows the \ spectrum of EXO 1256+281 with the ISGRI point extracted using this method. The \ spectrum is well fitted by a simple powerlaw with the photon index =1.63 0.07 and 0.5-10 keV flux 5 10^ 13 ergs cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper uses quasar B1256+281 behind Coma as a worked example for turbulent cluster magnetic-field simulations and ALP photon-survival probabilities.

quotes.quote: The quasars B1256+281 and SDSS J130001.48+275120.6 shining through Coma.

We take as an example the quasar B1256+281 located behind the Coma galaxy cluster. We use the same parameters for Coma's field as in our one-dimensional simulations, but simulate the turbulent field as follows: Generate a random vector potential field in Fourier space according to | A (k) |^2 |k|^ n . We allow k to take values from k_ min = 2 _ min to k_ max = 2 _ max . The phase of each component of A (k) is uniformly distributed between 0 and 2 . We use a Kolmogorov power spectrum corresponding to n = 17/3 , _ min = 2 kpc and _ min = 34 kpc . The Fourier space magnetic field is given by B (k) = i k A (k) . We find the real space field B ( x ) by taking the Fourier transform of B (k) and normalising the result to the correct amplitude based on distance from the cluster centre, as described in . We simulate B ( x ) on a 2000^3 grid with cell size 0.5 kpc. We then calculate the photon survival probability for five different sight-lines at a projected 240 kpc from the cluster centre, corresponding to the quasar's location.

sources.summary: The paper uses B1256+281 as the Coma1 quasar behind the Coma cluster, modeling its line-of-sight magnetic fields and fitting it with a power-law spectrum.

quotes.quote: This can be seen in Figure fig:mag_fields where we have plotted examples for the magnetic fields (absolute values) along the line of sight as well as the mean of all generated magnetic fields for the quasar B1256+281 which is located behind the Coma cluster.
The quasar B1256+281 behind Coma (Coma1).

A1795Sy1, A1367 and A1795Quasar have been fitted with a power law with additional absorption from neutral hydrogen, Coma1 only with a power law and Coma2 with a power law and a Fe K line ( E=6.4 keV ).

gmw_00002060.name: Q 0142-100
gmw_00002060.overview: Q 0142-100 is a doubly imaged gravitationally lensed quasar at a redshift of z = 2.719 [arXiv:1310.6569, arXiv:1307.2390]. The system is lensed by an early-type elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.49 [arXiv:1307.2390, arXiv:astro-ph/0612419], and its light output is dominated by intrinsic variability [arXiv:2107.06227].

sources.summary: The paper monitors Q0142-100, identified as the double quasar UM 673, deriving a 72 day image time delay and suggesting a possible microlensing event in the system.

quotes.quote: We present twelve years of monitoring of the gravitational lens Q0142-100 from the Teide Observatory. The data, taken from 1999 to 2010, comprise 105 observing nights with the IAC80 telescope. The application of the ^2 method to the dataset leads to a value of the time delay between both components of the system of 72 22 days (68 per cent confidence level), consistent within the uncertainties with the latest previous results. With this value in mind a possible microlensing event is detected in Q0142-100.

Q0142-100 (UM 673), discovered by mac82 , was classified as a gravitational lens system for the first time by sur87,sur88 . This double quasar shows two identical images ( z = 2.719 ) separated by 2.2 arcsecs with the lensing galaxy ( z = 0.49 ) placed among them ( sur88 , sme92 , eig07 ), see figure 1. The specially propitious configuration of Q0142-100, with large separation between both images, and their magnitudes ( m_R(A) 16.5 and m_R(B) 18.4 ) make this system adequate for a photometric monitoring from a medium-sized telescope.

sources.summary: The paper monitors the doubly imaged quasar UM673/Q0142-100, measuring seasonal flux and color changes and separating lens-galaxy light from the fainter component.

quotes.quote: In the current paper, we focus on / Q0142--100 (see Fig. fig:full ), a doubly imaged quasar discovered by during a high resolution imaging survey of HLQs (Highly Luminous Quasars) and subsequently studied by our team smette90,smette92,daulie93,nakos05 . reported a separation of 2.22 between the components ``A'' (brighter) and ``B'' (fainter), and found their V magnitudes to be 16.9 and 19.1 respectively, at a redshift z=2.719 . The redshift of the sensibly fainter ( R=19.2 ) lensing galaxy, located very close to the ``B'' component, was derived to be z=0.49 , and the time delay between the two lensed components was estimated around 7 weeks.

Our results show for the brightest lensed component some significant decrease in flux between the first two seasons ( +0.09 / +0.11 / +0.05 mag) and a subsequent increase during the following ones ( 0.11 / 0.11 / 0.10 mag) in the V/R/i spectral bands, respectively.
