sources.summary: The low-excitation FRII galaxy 3C 388 has diffuse cluster-scale X-ray emission, unusual radio structure, and cooling-flow fits that still require a nuclear point source.

quotes.quote: 3C 388 The X-ray images of this low-excitation FRII radio galaxy show a significant amount of diffuse emission. The source is known to lie in a poor cluster, with a number of nearby galaxies (Prestage Peacock 1988). In the radio the object has an unusual structure. Its one-sided bright jet and diffuse hot spots might put it in the class of jetted doubles discussed earlier (section jetted ), but the presence of two distinct spectral regimes in its lobes suggests that it may be a restarting source (Roettiger \ 1994). model fits are tabulated in Table profile and show the X-ray emission to be dominated by an extended component. No model without a point source is an acceptable fit to the data, however.

Gas of the density implied by the fitted model should be rapidly cooling, with a mass deposition rate of 30 M_ yr ^ 1 . When we fit with cooling-flow models, using a temperature T 3 keV outside the cooling radius, the lack of a strong peak in the central regions (as with 3C 295) means that no model with p ^ 1 is a good fit; we find acceptable fits for models with a weaker dependence of p upon , for example with p ^ 0.5 , ^ 0.75 , T ^ 0.25 , =0.5 , r_ core = 13 arcsec. The cooling radius for this model is 9 arcsec and the cooling time at this radius is about 5 10^9 yr. A point-like nuclear component is still required, containing 66 19 counts.

sources.summary: The paper reports 90 GHz detections of 3C 388 jet and lobe-related emission, with jet spectral curvature and lobe steepening compatible with spectral-aging models.

quotes.quote: Jet-related emission is clearly detected in three of the newly observed sources, 3C 388, 3C 401, and 3C 438. This is the first time that FRII jets have been detected at these high radio frequencies. The three detected sources are all part of the class of low-excitation radio galaxies with prominent jets and diffuse hotspots, possibly lying in rich environments, identified by Hardcastle \ (1997).

3C 20 (as already discussed in Hardcastle Looney 2001) and 3C 388 show clear spectral steepening away from the hotspots. In 3C 388, the only other source where we have multi-frequency data on the lobes readily available, we find that the same is true -- a J-P model provides a good fit to the data from the W lobe provided that the injection index is 0.7 .

sources.summary: 3C 388 is oriented along its host major axis, lacks detected ISM, and has radio lobes that blow cavities and may shape perpendicular X-ray ridges.

quotes.quote: 3C 388 ( z=0.091 ) : 3C 388 is a radio galaxy oriented along the major axis of its host, but we detect no ISM in the X-ray exposure due to the strength of the surrounding ICM. The radio galaxy is comparable to the size of X-ray ICM isophotes elongated in its direction, which kraft06 attribute to the influence of the radio galaxy on its surroundings. The radio lobes have blown cavities in the X-ray emission, but near the core of the radio galaxy, the X-ray isophotes are elongated in a direction perpendicular to the radio lobes. Although much of this asymmetry is likely due to the cavities eating out the sides of a spheroid where the radio galaxy overlaps the core region (i.e. it is a physical structure but may not precede the radio galaxy), the western radio lobe seems to bend arounnd the ridge. Since the fainter portions of the radio lobes may be older plasma evolving buoyantly in the ICM (the jet in the western lobe is farther to the south), the ridge may also be influencing the shape of the radio galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C388 as a restarted radio galaxy/AGN, mapping its low-frequency radio spectrum, finding unusual lobe spectral structure, and estimating an 80 Myr age and roughly 60 percent duty cycle.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C388 has long been claimed to be a peculiar example of an AGN with multi-epoch activity because it shows a very sharp discontinuity in the GHz spectral index distribution of its lobes. We present here for the first time a spatially resolved study of the radio spectrum of 3C388 down to MHz frequencies aimed at investigating the radiative age of the source and constraining its duty cycle. We used new low-frequency observations at 144 MHz performed with the Low Frequency Array and at 350 MHz performed with the Very Large Array that we combined with archival data at higher frequencies (614, 1400, and 4850 MHz).

Using single-injection radiative models, we compute that the total source age is 80 Myr and that the duty cycle is about t_ on /t_ tot 60 , which is enough to prevent the intracluster medium from cooling, according to X-ray estimates. While to date the radio spectral distribution of 3C388 remains a rare case among radio galaxies, multi-frequency surveys performed with new-generation instruments will soon allow us to investigate whether more sources with the same characteristics exist.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 388 as a restarted radio galaxy whose lobe spectral regions trace old remnant and recent activity, with LOFAR data yielding age and duty-cycle estimates.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C 388 was, until recently, the only object where two regions within the lobes with very different spectral properties were found . This was taken as an indication of two epochs of activity---one representing remnant emission from a past phase of activity and one representing a recent restarted phase. This scenario was confirmed by our study expanding the frequency range using LOFAR data in which we derive a total source age of <80 Myr and a quite fast duty cycle of about t_ on /t_ tot 60 . The analysis also shows that mixing of electrons from the two phases may also be happening, complicating the age estimates.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 3C 388 has two bright east-west radio lobes in LoTSS imaging, no clear central compact component, and lobes coincident with X-ray depressions.

quotes.quote: 3C 388 : Similar to as observed with the OMT by mackay69 and branson72 , the LoTSS map shows two bright radio lobes located towards the east and west of the core of the cluster. No central compact component can be clearly detected. Both of the radio lobes are coincident with X-ray depressions kraft06 , although the low sensitivity of the X-ray observations does not enable these cavities to be studied in detail.

gmw_00002076.name: JADES GN + 189.23669 + 62.20247
gmw_00002076.overview: JADES GN + 189.23669 + 62.20247 is a galaxy at a redshift of z = 5.200 that lies well below the star-forming main sequence [arXiv:2311.04270]. It exhibits a strong Balmer break, a mass-weighted age of 390 Myr, and belongs to the JADES GN OD 5.191 overdensity [arXiv:2311.04270].

sources.summary: The paper highlights JADES GN + 189.23669 + 62.20247 as a z=5.200 outlier below the star-forming main sequence, with a strong Balmer break, 390 Myr age, and membership in JADES GN OD 5.191.

quotes.quote: There is one interesting outlier well below the empirical star-forming main sequence in the left panel of Figure fig:Figure_06 (JADES GN + 189.23669 + 62.20247 at z_ spec = 5.200 ). With an inferred stellar mass of M_ = 10^ 9.2 M_ , the observed photometry suggests a strong Balmer break, implying a mass-weighted age of t_ MW = 390 Myr . This galaxy is part of the largest overdensity identified here in terms of total number of galaxies and inferred halo mass (JADES GN OD 5.191 ), with an angular (physical) separation of 0.6 arcmin ( 1.5 cMpc) from the well-known sub-millimeter galaxy HDF850.1.

gmw_00002077.name: 6C*0020+440
gmw_00002077.overview: 6C*0020+440 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.988 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127, arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. It features strong Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127] and an extremely faint K-band counterpart situated between its two radio lobes [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 6C*0020+440 as a z=2.988 radio galaxy, supported by Ly and probable CIV emission and by a K-band magnitude consistent with that redshift.

quotes.quote: 6C*0020+440 A blind spectrum was taken pointed at the mid-point of the two hotspots where there appears to be a faint K band identification. Strong Ly emission and probable CIV 1549 emission point to a radio galaxy at z=2.988 . The K- band magnitude ( K = 18.9 in a 5 arcsec aperture) is also consistent with the source being at this redshift.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 6C*0020+440 as a high-redshift radio source with an extremely faint plausible K-band counterpart between its lobes, noting nearby bright objects may contaminate aperture photometry.

quotes.quote: The reduced images are shown in Fig. fig:kband_images with the radio contours overlaid. ht ht ht 6C*0020+440 ( z = 2.988 ) The extremely faint plausible ID, (a) in Fig. fig:kband_images , is situated between the two radio lobes but closer to the eastern lobe. There are also two bright objects close to the radio structure. Both object (b) 2.5 arcsec to the north of our ID, and object () 3 arcsec to the south-west of this identification may contribute some flux to the magnitude in apertures > 3 arcsec.

gmw_00002078.name: 6C*0031+403
gmw_00002078.overview: The high-redshift radio galaxy 6C*0031+403 is located at a redshift of z = 1.619 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. It features a bright optical counterpart co-spatial with its flat-spectrum core and exhibits a one-sided radio jet [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper reports a bright counterpart at the flat-spectrum core of 6C*0031+403, notes its unusual one-sided jet, and suggests it may be a reddened quasar.

quotes.quote: 6C*0031+403 ( z = 1.619 ) Our image shows a bright ID co-spatial with the flat-spectrum core. The radio structure of this source is somewhat unusual for the 6C* sample: a one-sided jet emerges from the bright core, suggestive of a jet disrupted on one side of the source. We speculate that this object might be a reddened quasar with an additional non-stellar component to the light, due to the unusually bright magnitude for a source at this redshift, although there is no evidence of any broad line component from our spectroscopic observations.

gmw_00002079.name: 6C*0032+412
gmw_00002079.overview: 6C*0032+412 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.658 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. Infrared spectroscopic observations reveal that emission lines contribute 50 percent of its flux, with hints that the light is extended along the radio axis [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper says 6C*0032+412 has substantial [OIII] line contamination, uncertain UKIRT photometry, possible radio-axis extension, and astrometry uncertain enough to explain the offset.

quotes.quote: 6C*0032+412 ( z = 3.658 ) Our infrared spectroscopic observations of this source (Jarvis et al. 2001) reveals that 50 of the flux is contributed by the [ OIII ] emission lines at 4959 and 5007 . The errors on this value are large because of the tentative nature of the UKIRT ID, and consequently inaccurate photometry. There are hints that the light is extended along the radio axis, probably due to significant contamination by emission lines. The astrometry for this image is accurate to 1.5 arcsec and uncertain enough to reconcile the apparent displacement of the ID with the radio emission.

gmw_00002080.name: 6C*0050+419
gmw_00002080.overview: 6C*0050+419 is a high-redshift radio galaxy with a tentative redshift of 1.748 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. Deep imaging reveals multiple faint candidate identifications near its compact radio emission, including faint blobs of emission located to the south and southwest of the radio peak [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper discusses multiple faint candidate identifications for 6C*0050+419 around the compact radio emission, including southern and southwestern blobs seen in K and I band images.

quotes.quote: 6C*0050+419 ( z = 1.748 ?) There is a faint object to the south of the peak of the compact radio emission (a), which is most plausibly the ID. Deep imaging with the Keck telescope (appendix A) also reveals a faint blob of emission to the south of the peak of the radio emission. There also appears to be a faint blob of emission slightly to the north of the radio peak in the image, although there is no visible counterpart we cannot rule this out as being associated with the radio source. There is also a faint blob to the south-west (object `b') in both our K and I band images co-spatial with the faint radio component in our 8.4 GHz map, which could also be the ID, and has a magnitude of K 19.5 (8 arcsec aperture).

gmw_00002081.name: 6C*0133+486
gmw_00002081.overview: 6C*0133+486 is a high-redshift radio galaxy with a tentative redshift of z = 1.029 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. There are two possible optical identifications for this source along the line of its radio jet, with the brightest component favored as the host [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper describes two possible identifications for 6C*0133+486, favoring the brighter component as the host from the K-z diagram while noting the northern object remains possible.

quotes.quote: 6C*0133+486 ( z = 1.029 ?) There are two possible identifications for this radio source, although these are along the line of the radio jet and both may be associated with the radio galaxy. Our tentative redshift of z = 1.029 from the brightest component (a) leads us to conclude, from the K-z diagram, that this is the host galaxy, although this is still 0.8 magnitudes fainter than the best-fit line through the 6CE/6C* K-z diagram (Fig. fig:kzdiag ). However, object (b) 2 arcsec to the north cannot be ruled out as the host and further spectroscopic observations will be needed to confirm the ID and redshift of this radio source.

gmw_00002082.name: 6C*0136+388
gmw_00002082.overview: The galaxy 6C*0136+388 is identified at the position of its radio core [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. Its K-band magnitude is consistent with the K-z relation at a tentative redshift of 1.108 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper places the identification of 6C*0136+388 at the radio core, notes the bright counterpart hides the core in the image, and says its magnitude matches the K-z relation.

quotes.quote: 6C*0136+388 ( z = 1.108 ?) The ID of this source lies at the radio core. The radio core is not visible in the image presented in Fig. fig:kband_images because of the bright ID; the peak in the radio emission is represented by the white cross. The magnitude is also consistent with the K-z relation at this redshift.

gmw_00002083.name: 6C*0139+344
gmw_00002083.overview: 6C*0139+344 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.637 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. Optical imaging reveals a fainter northern object identified as the radio galaxy, situated near a foreground galaxy at z = 0.37 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper finds a foreground galaxy near 6C*0139+344, identifies the fainter northern object as the radio galaxy, and notes no counterpart at the former radio-core candidate position.

quotes.quote: 6C*0139+344 ( z = 1.637 ) Our optical spectra reveal a nearby foreground galaxy at z = 0.37 which could be contributing to our measured magnitude. Our image reveals two galaxies towards what appears to be a steep-spectrum radio lobe to the north. We take the fainter of these (a) to be the radio galaxy with the z = 0.37 foreground galaxy (b) approximately 2.5 arcsec to the north, which is in agreement with our astrometry from the 2D spectrum. There are no objects in our images which are coincident with the faint radio component in our 4.9 GHz map at 01 39 25.87 +34 27 02.5 (B1950) which we originally thought to be the core.

gmw_00002084.name: 6C*0155+424
gmw_00002084.overview: 6C*0155+424 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.513 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. The host galaxy has two nearby companion galaxies of similar brightness, and the system exhibits faint diffuse emission indicative of an ongoing major merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper treats 6C*0155+424 as an uncertain radio-galaxy identification in a likely interacting system, with nearby similarly bright companions and diffuse emission suggestive of a major merger.

quotes.quote: 6C*0155+424 ( z = 0.513 ) The R- (Jarvis 2000) and images of this radio source reveal three optically bright sources that could each plausibly be the radio galaxy. The magnitudes of all three point towards a low redshift ( z < 1 ) and our optical spectroscopy revealed that the ID closest to the centre of the radio emission has a redshift z = 0.513 and is probably the host galaxy on positional grounds. The nearest neighbour to this source is 1.5 arcsec to the north-west, and the similar magnitudes suggest that these two objects could be in the process of merging.

This also has a similarly bright magnitude and is also plausibly a true companion galaxy. There is faint diffuse emission surrounding the two closest objects, and the third has faint emission reaching towards the other two, which is also indicative that this system may be undergoing a major merger.

gmw_00002085.name: 6C*0158+315
gmw_00002085.overview: 6C*0158+315 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.505 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127, arXiv:astro-ph/0106130]. The galaxy is co-spatial with a compact radio source, though it could potentially be the core of a larger triple source [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127, arXiv:astro-ph/0106130].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 6C*0158+315 as the radio galaxy counterpart, gives a z=1.505 redshift from several lines, and notes possible exclusion if it is part of a huge triple source.

quotes.quote: 6C*0158+315 It was unclear from our radio map whether this source was a discrete object or a hot-spot of a larger source. In our R and K band imaging we find an object co-spatial with the peak of the radio emission which we now take to be the radio galaxy. Definite lines corresponding to MgII 2799 , [ NeIV ] 2424 and CII ] 2326 confirm this source at z=1.505 . The NVSS radio map shows that the radio component associated with the optical ID could conceivably be the core of a large ( 14 arcmin) triple source, if this is the case then this source should be excluded from the final sample, but if this were true the linear size of the source would be huge ( 7 Mpc for _ M =1 , _ =0 ).

sources.summary: The paper reports a counterpart co-spatial with compact radio emission in 6C*0158+315, revising an earlier hotspot interpretation while allowing a larger triple-source possibility.

quotes.quote: 6C*0158+315 ( z = 1.505 ) We find a ID co-spatial with the compact radio source. This was initially thought to be a hot-spot associated with a larger source. However, the imaging data presented in this paper suggest that it is indeed a discrete source, although a triple source spanning 14 arcmin cannot be ruled out completely (but see Jarvis et al. 2001).

gmw_00002086.name: NGC 1515
gmw_00002086.overview: NGC 1515 is a galaxy located within a loose galaxy group [arXiv:1312.2685]. Observations reveal that the galaxy is strongly HI-deficient and features a truncated, slightly warped HI disk [arXiv:1312.2685].

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 1515 among strongly HI-deficient low-density-environment galaxies, observes it with ATCA, and reports a truncated, slightly warped HI disk.

quotes.quote: This sample of deficient galaxies is particularly interesting since 4 of the sample galaxies (IC 1993, NGC 1473, NGC 1515, NGC 6808) are residing in lose galaxy groups one galaxy is in a galaxy triplet (ESO 009-G 010) and one galaxy is in the field (NGC 6699). deficient galaxies are well known and studied in dense galaxy clusters and in compact groups (e.g Giovanelli1985, Kenney2004, Vollmer2001, Verdes-Montenegro2001 ), but only a handful of them is known in low density galaxy groups ( Chamaraux2004, Kilborn2005, Sengupta2006 ). We observed each galaxy for 10 hours with the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) 750 meter configuration. Our preliminary results show that one of the six galaxies, NGC 1515, appears to have a truncated and slightly warped \ disk (Figure fig:HI-deficient-galaxies ).

gmw_00002087.name: NGC 6808
gmw_00002087.overview: NGC 6808 is an HI-deficient galaxy located within a loose galaxy group [arXiv:1312.2685]. It exhibits a slightly warped stellar disk and a possible HI disk extension, suggesting that tidal interactions with other group members are stripping its gas [arXiv:1312.2685].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 6808 as HI-deficient in a loose group, observes it with ATCA, and links its warped stellar and HI structure to tidal interactions.

quotes.quote: This sample of deficient galaxies is particularly interesting since 4 of the sample galaxies (IC 1993, NGC 1473, NGC 1515, NGC 6808) are residing in lose galaxy groups one galaxy is in a galaxy triplet (ESO 009-G 010) and one galaxy is in the field (NGC 6699). deficient galaxies are well known and studied in dense galaxy clusters and in compact groups (e.g Giovanelli1985, Kenney2004, Vollmer2001, Verdes-Montenegro2001 ), but only a handful of them is known in low density galaxy groups ( Chamaraux2004, Kilborn2005, Sengupta2006 ). We observed each galaxy for 10 hours with the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) 750 meter configuration.

Another of our sample galaxies, NGC 6808 apperas to have a slightly warped stellar disk, based on its Hubble Space Telescope (HST) image and a possible extension of its \ disk pointing towards the centre of the galaxy group it belongs to (Figure fig:HI-deficient-galaxies ). This suggest that the main gas stripping mechanism for this galaxy may be tidal interactions with group member galaxies.

gmw_00002088.name: 2WHSP J103118.4+505335
gmw_00002088.overview: 2WHSP J103118.4+505335 is a blazar [arXiv:astro-ph/0501126, arXiv:2005.11581] located at a redshift of z=0.361 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501126]. It is classified as a promising candidate for TeV-energy observations [arXiv:2005.11581], having previously exhibited a short outburst phase followed by a return to a quiescent state [arXiv:astro-ph/0501126].

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1028+511 as a z=0.361 blazar observed by Chandra LETG after an outburst, yielding bright soft X-ray spectra with WHIM absorption detections.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1028+511 is a blazar at z=0.361 . It was observed under our Chandra TOO program, with the Chandra LETG, following a short outburst phase (3-days), on 2004, March 11. Although 1ES 1028+511 was back close to its 'quiescent' level when Chandra pointed at it, 1ES 1028+511 turned out to be a bright soft X-ray source for such a high redshift ( F_ 0.5-2 keV = 1.1 mCrab). The 150 ks HRC-LETG spectrum of 1ES 1028+511 contains 80-100 CPREs in the 21 < ( ) 50 interval, enough to detect at 3 N _ O VII 1.4 10^ 16 (1+z)^ 2 cm ^ 2 at z 0.361 , and 4.1 times lower C V columns, N _ C V 3.4 10^ 15 (1+z)^ 2 cm ^ 2 at z 0.269 (at > 51 i.e. z > 0.269 for C V _ 1s 2p , the spectrum is heavily affected by the presence of the HRC plate gap).

We detect two strong lines (3.5 and 4.4 ) at = 28.740 0.038 \ and = 48.816 0.040 that we identify as O VII _ 1s 2p from a WHIM system at z=0.330 0.002 , and C V _ 1s 2p from a WHIM system at z=0.212 0.001 (Figure 2 (a) and (b) respectively; Nicastro et al., 2005, in preparation: N05).

sources.summary: The paper selects 2WHSP J103118.4+505335 as a Class I TeV follow-up candidate whose VERITAS limit already constrains the best-fit hadronic model.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1028+511 Source 2WHSP J103118.4+505335, 3FGL J1031.2+5053, also known with the name 1ES 1028+511, shown in Figure fig_sources , is a promising candidate for observation by CTA and potentially MAGIC and VERITAS, based on our Fermi analysis. This source has been observed by VERITAS for 24.1 hours, yielding an upper limit of of 7.7 10^ 12 cm^ 1 s^ 1 erg^ 1 2016AJ....151..142A . Interestingly, for this source the VERITAS upper limit constrains the hadronic model for the level of flux predicted with the best fit index. Thus additional observations of this source may be very sensitive to or otherwise very constraining of the hadronic model.

gmw_00002089.name: 2WHSP J141756.5+254324
gmw_00002089.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2WHSP J141756.5+254324 is a BL Lacertae object and a blazar [arXiv:2005.11581]. It exhibits a very low variability index and is classified as a possible extreme high-synchrotron-peaked source [arXiv:2005.11581].

sources.summary: The paper presents 2WHSP J141756.5+254324 as a low-variability TeV candidate with firm redshift, possible extreme-HSP nature, and prior VERITAS limits.

quotes.quote: RBS 1366 With a detection significance of 12 , a very low variability index, and a firm redshift determination 2012ApJS..203...21A , 2WHSP J141756.5+254324, 3FGL J1417.8+2540, also known as RBS 1366, is also a promising candidate for detecting with TeV instruments. The large uncertainties in the Fermi LAT analysis however mean that we cannot conclusively determine whether the source will be detectable. Under optimistic assumptions (upper 1 uncertainty range) it is a good candidate for observation by CTA or VERITAS and also possibly by MAGIC. A differential upper limit of 1.7 10^ 11 cm^ 1 s^ 1 erg^ 1 at 327 GeV was calculated for this source by VERITAS based on 10 hours of observations, but is not sufficient to constrain the hadronic origin model 2016AJ....151..142A . With synchrotron peak frequency at _ S = 17.4 , this source is possibly an extreme-HSP, and thus interesting to study at VHE even if it is purely leptonic, for the purpose of furthering our knowledge of this, small and extreme source population.

gmw_00002090.name: RBS 621
gmw_00002090.overview: RBS 621 is a blazar and BL Lacertae object with a low variability index [arXiv:2005.11581]. Its redshift remains uncertain with a lower limit of z > 0.26 [arXiv:2005.11581], as multiple spectroscopic observations have yielded only featureless spectra [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RBS 621 as a blazar with high LAT significance, low variability, uncertain redshift, and strong promise for H.E.S.S. or CTA detection.

quotes.quote: RBS 621 The blazar RBS 621 (3FGL J0506.9-5435, 2WHSP J050657.7-543503), with LAT detection significance of around 23.0 and low variability TS _V = 40.6 , is another source promising for TeV detection and for the detection of the UHECR hadronic signature. The redshift is uncertain with a lower limit of z > 0.26 . To the best of our knowledge this source has not yet been observed with H.E.S.S., but it is our most promising source for detection with a 50h exposure if the true redshift is close to the lower limit and certainly promising for observations with CTA.

sources.summary: The source had a previous featureless spectrum; new high-S/N SALT/RSS spectroscopy is also featureless, leaving no redshift measurement from these observations.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0505 546 A featureless EFOSC spectrum with intermediate S/N has been reported by Mase13 . We observed the source with SALT/RSS for an exposure time of 2340 sec. Our SALT/RSS spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , third row panel, on the left) has a high S/N ( 120) and it is featureless.

gmw_00002091.name: NGC 5474
gmw_00002091.overview: NGC 5474 is an asymmetric, late-type peculiar dwarf spiral galaxy featuring three arms on one side of its center [arXiv:2201.07773, arXiv:2206.03427]. It is a companion of M101, with which it has undergone a recent gravitational interaction that likely offset its bulge-like structure [arXiv:2109.06430, arXiv:2311.08160].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5474 as M101's lower-mass companion, measuring its H II regions, offset bulge center, and star-formation indicators alongside M101 environments.

quotes.quote: We perform a statistical analysis of the \ to FUV flux ratios in H 2 regions located in three different environments: M101's inner disk, M101's outer disk, and M101's lower mass companion galaxy NGC 5474. We find that, once bulk radial trends in extinction are taken into account, both the median and scatter in \ in H 2 regions are invariant across all of these environments. Also, using Starburst99 models, we are able to qualitatively reproduce the distributions of \ throughout these different environments using a standard Kroupa IMF, hence we find no need to invoke truncations in the upper mass end of the IMF to explain the young star-forming regions in the M101 Group even at extremely low surface density. This implies that star formation in low density environments differs from star formation in high density environments only by intensity and not by cloud-to-cloud physics.

It should be noted here that NGC 5474 has a strongly offset bulge vanderhulst79, kornreich98 , hence the definition of its ``center'' is not entirely clear. We define its center as the centroid of the circular outer isophotes (at 180 , or 6 kpc) on our on-band mosaic, which is very close to the kinematic center of its (strangely regular) H 1 velocity field vanderhulst79 . This choice does not affect the qualitative behavior of the radial profiles, however the flux profile does show more scatter with radius when centered on the bulge. This implies that the isophotal center is the more appropriate choice regarding star formation in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5474 as M101's likely massive interacting satellite and reports a high-equivalent-width outlying H II-region candidate projected 8.8 kpc from it.

quotes.quote: M101 was chosen for this survey because its nearby distance enables its properties to be studied in detail mihos2012,mihos2013,mihos2018,watkins2017 . M101 is also currently interacting with its satellite population, likely its massive satellite NGC 5474 as evidenced by its asymmetric disk beale1969,rownd1994,waller1997 . Given that interacting systems often display extended or outlying star-forming regions, the M101 Group could be an excellent case study to explore the conditions under which extended intragroup star formation is triggered.

Finally, we turn to the source associated with NGC 5474 with the highest EW in the entire sample. This source has an H EW of 422 43 putting it firmly in the realm of H emitting outlying H 2 regions as defined by . It also has moderately strong H and [ O 3 ] EWs, 71 22 and 282 37 , respectively. It has a projected separation from NGC 5474 of ;;265 ( 8.8 ). Given its structural and photometric properties, this source is likely an outlying H 2 region associated with NGC 5474, making it the best (and perhaps only) such candidate discovered in our survey.

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs NGC 5474's star-formation history, finding an old but recently active off-centre bulge-like component, a SW over-density, gasping disc activity, and synchronized recent bursts.

quotes.quote: We study the resolved stellar populations and derive the star formation history of NGC 5474, a peculiar star-forming dwarf galaxy at a distance of 7 Mpc, using Hubble Space Telescope Advanced Camera for Surveys data from the Legacy Extragalactic UV Survey (LEGUS) program.

The off-centre bulge-like structure, suggested to constitute the bulge of the galaxy, is dominated by star formation (SF) activity initiated 14 Gyr ago and lasted at least up to 1 Gyr ago. Nevertheless, this component shows clear evidence of prolonged SF activity (lasting until 10 Myr ago). We estimate the total stellar mass of the bulge-like structure to be (5.0 0.3) 10^ 8 . Such a mass is consistent with published suggestions that this structure is in fact an independent system orbiting around and not within NGC 5474's disc.

The stellar over-density located to the South-West of the bulge-like structure shows a significant SF event older than 1 Gyr, while it is characterised by two recent peaks of SF, around 10 and 100 Myr ago. In the last Gyr, the behavior of the stellar disc is consistent with what is known in the literature as `gasping'. The synchronised burst at 10-35 Myr in all components might hint to the recent gravitational interaction between the stellar bulge-like structure and the disc of NGC 5474.

sources.summary: NGC 5474 is presented as a likely recent interaction partner of M101, with intermediate-velocity HI between the galaxies and a slight asymmetry in its own HI profile.

quotes.quote: Slice E cuts first through the nuclei of both NGC 5474 and M101, and shows the presence of diffuse HI gas located between the galaxies. This gas is at velocities intermediate between NGC 5474's systemic velocity and the velocity of the southeastern edge of M101's disk, suggestive of material drawn out of M101 due to an interaction with NGC 5474. Indeed, a cut along the kinematic major axis of NGC 5474 (slice F) shows a slight kinematic asymmetry as well; there is more gas on the lower velocity side of the profile, at similar velocity ( =240 ) to the diffuse gas found between the galaxies in slice E.

NGC 5474 is a more luminous system (M _V =-18.4 ; de Vaucouleurs 1991) and sports a strongly asymmetric disk (Rownd 1994, Kornreich 1998), but shows no sign of any extended tidal debris in the optical (Mihos 2012). Previous single dish mapping of the M101 system hinted at the possibility of an HI bridge between NGC 5474 and M101 (HW79), but subsequent HI synthesis imaging has shown no sign of any HI tidal features around NGC 5474 (Rownd 1994, Kornreich 2000) which might trace a previous interaction. Our data strengthen the case for a past interaction between M101 and NGC 5474. NGC 5474 itself shows a slight kinematic asymmetry as well (slice E), with more extended HI emission at lower velocities similar to that on the southern edge of M101's disk.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5474 as M101’s luminous companion, finding no real bridge at FAST resolution but arguing weak interaction and M101’s tail connect to it.

quotes.quote: For the other companion NGC 5474, it is more luminous than NGC 5477. Huchtmeier1979 and Mihos2012 detected some diffuse gas between M101 and NGC 5474 in a column-density map and a PV diagram, respectively, strengthening the case for a recent interaction between the two galaxies. While our high sensitivity observation with a relatively high resolution (2.9 ^ ) did not detect the diffuse bridge connecting M101 with NGC 5474 in a column-density map (see Figure Fig:M101_M_HI-DSS ) in a PV diagram (see the panel b of Figure Fig:M101-PV ).

We did not detect a bridge between the two galaxies, but identify a slightly extended disk for NGC 5474. It means that the two galaxies have undergone only a weak interaction, just like the judgement of Garner2021 .

Here the tails detected in M101 gives a reliable evidence for M101 interaction with NGC 5474. Thus, we conclude that the extra-planar gas (three clouds) and the tail detected in the M101's disk are likely to origin from a minor interaction with the companion NGC 5474.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5474 as an asymmetric, lower-mass spiral with reduced H2 content and quasi-regular young-star spacings of roughly 390-860 pc across its arms.

quotes.quote: NGC 5474 is an asymmetric spiral galaxy with three arms located on one side of the centre (Fig. figure:map5474 ). Well known NGC 6946 is seem a rather flocculent (Fig. figure:map6946 ), however it has a regular spiral density wave kendall2011,ghosh2016 . Two of the three galaxies, NGC 895 and NGC 5474, are on the list of elmegreen1983 . NGC 5474 is about 10 times less massive and fainter than two other targets (see Table table:sample ). It is similar to LMC in terms of mass and luminosity. The H i mass in NGC 5474 is also approximately 10 times less than that in NGC 6946 rownd1994,walter2008 , however, H _2 to-H i mass ratio is equal to 0.86 in NGC 6946 leroy2009 , but it does not reach 0.06 in NGC 5474 wilson2012 .

Only five local maxima of brightness were found in the inner short spiral arm of NGC 5474 (S1; see Figs. figure:map5474 , figure:profile5474 ). The first four of them form a regular chain with a separation 430 60 pc, and the distance between the fourth and fifth local maxima of brightness is twice as large and equal to 860 30 pc (Figs. figure:separate , figure:hist ). The Fourier analysis of a sample of five objects can only be formal; it shows the selected scale of 420 pc with a FAP <15 (Fig. figure:power ). Distribution of star formation regions in S2 looks rather irregular (Figs. figure:separate , figure:hist ). Some regularity seems to be observed in the inner part of this spiral arm at s=1.5-5 kpc ( =1.8-2.7 kpc), where we found six local maxima of brightness. Five of them are located at a distance 660 60 pc from each other, and one pair has a double separation l=1.14 0.05 kpc (Fig. figure:separate ). The four most distant star formation regions from this sample were identified by elmegreen1983 as a regular chain of four H ii regions (see Fig. figure:map5474 ). Power spectral density of the function p(s) for S2 never reaches the level of 25 FAP. The PSD maximum at l 620 pc probably reflects the fact of quasi-regularity in distribution of star formation regions at s=1.5-5 kpc in the S2 arm (Fig. figure:power ). Unlike the S2, the spiral arm S3 in the galaxy shows a regular distribution of the young stellar population along the arm.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5474 as M101's companion and models its retrograde grazing passage through M101's outer disk as the cause of observed asymmetries and plume structure.

quotes.quote: Our model describes a grazing but relatively close ( 14 kpc) passage of the companion galaxy NGC 5474 through M101's outer disk approximately 200 Myr ago. The passage is retrograde for both disks, which yields a relatively strong gravitational response while suppressing the formation of long tidal tails.

We have constructed the first N-body simulation to capture the gravitational encounter between M101 and its companion NGC 5474. The encounter involves a grazing, low inclination passage of NGC 5474 through M101's outer disk ( R_ peri 14 kpc), with closest approach occurring approximately 200 Myr ago. The interaction was a retrograde passage for both galaxies, taking place largely in the plane of the sky as observed from our vantage point. Our simulation reproduces many of the features observed in the M101 system today: including the projected distance and velocity of the two galaxies, M101's overall lopsided asymmetry, the morphology of M101's NE spiral arm and NE Plume, and the sharp edge to the west side of M101's disk. We also roughly match the 200 -- 300 Myr timescale for the encounter inferred from stellar age constraints in M101's NE Plume, and show that this stellar population was likely formed in a burst of star formation triggered at the contact point when the two disks originally collided.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5474 as an asymmetric three-armed Scd galaxy whose inner, middle, and far arms contain regular star-forming chains at several characteristic distances.

quotes.quote: The first study gusev2022 considered the distribution of the young stellar galaxy in the spiral arms of the Scd galaxies NGC 895, NGC 5474, and NGC 6946.

NGC 895 is a typical two-arm grand design galaxy; NGC 5474 is an asymmetric galaxy with three arms on one side of the center; the four-armed NGC 6946 appears to be flocculent, although its spirals are of a wave nature kendall2011,ghosh2016 (Fig. figure:fig1 ).

In the short inner arm of NGC 5474, found a chain of five star-forming regions forming a regular sequence with =430 pc and 2 =860 pc. In the middle arm of the galaxy, six star-forming regions constituted a regular chain with characteristic distances between the neighbors of 660 pc and 1.15 kpc. This chain was previously noted in . Two characteristic distances were also found in the far spiral arm: 390 pc and the doubled 740 pc. The latter was also confirmed by the Fourier analysis data, which gave a value of 750 pc (Figs. figure:fig2 , figure:fig3 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5474 as M101’s lower-mass dwarf companion, included in the DIG analysis and implicated as the likely driver of M101’s recent outer-disk starburst.

quotes.quote: We examine the connection between diffuse ionised gas (DIG), regions, and field O and B stars in the nearby spiral M101 and its dwarf companion NGC 5474 using ultra-deep H narrow-band imaging and archival GALEX UV imaging. We find a strong correlation between DIG H surface brightness and the incident ionising flux leaked from the nearby regions, which we reproduce well using simple Cloudy simulations.

The group is rather sparse, containing only the massive ( M _ * )=10.6 ; munozmateos15 face-on spiral M101 (NGC 5457), its lower mass ( M _ * )=9.1 ; munozmateos15 companion NGC 5474, the irregular star-forming dwarf NGC 5477, and a handful of much fainter satellite candidates, most only recently identified muller17 . Indeed, a recent survey using the Hubble Space Telescope found that M101's satellite population is very sparse, with roughly half the number of low-mass companions as the MW to a limit of M_ V =-7.7 bennet20 . However, this low group mass, and consequent low velocity dispersion, should allow for more impactful tidal interactions between the group members negroponte83 . Integrated light mihos13 and resolved stellar mihos18 photometry of M101's outer disk has illustrated the impact of this on the star formation history (SFH): a burst of star formation which peaked 300---400 Myr ago in M101's outer disk. Follow-up simulations demonstrate that the most massive companion, NGC 5474, is the most likely culprit linden22 .

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 5474 in its six-galaxy LBT outer-disk sample, finding marginally resolved star clusters beyond R_25 that trace H I gas and imply low-level cluster formation.

quotes.quote: Expanding on the pilot project of , we present deep ( 27.5 mag V band point-source limiting magnitude) U \ and V band imaging of six galaxies: IC 4182, NGC 3351, NGC 4736, NGC 4826, NGC 5474, and NGC 6503. We find that the outer disk of each galaxy is populated with marginally-resolved star clusters with masses 10^3 M_ and ages up to 1 Gyr (masses and ages are limited by the depth of our imaging and uncertainties are large given how photometry can be strongly affected by the presence or absence of a few stars in such low mass systems), and that they are typically found out to at least 2 R_ 25 but sometimes as far as 3 to 4 R_ 25 - even beyond the apparent H 1 disk. The mean rate of cluster formation for 1 R_ 25 R 1.5R_ 25 is at least one every 2.5 Myr and the clusters are spatially correlated with the H 1 , most strongly with higher density gas near the periphery of the optical disk and with lower density neutral gas at the H 1 disk periphery.

We present new observations of six galaxies (IC 4182, NGC 3351, NGC 4736, NGC 4826, NGC 5474, NGC 6503) with the LBC-Blue imaging camera on the LBT on the dates listed in Table tab:gals . These systems comprise a representative sample of nearby disk galaxies by spanning a range of physical disk sizes. All are nearby ( D 15 Mpc) and only two, NGC 4826 and NGC 6503, are seen at high inclination ( > 60^ ).

sources.summary: The paper evaluates NGC 5474 as a likely M101 companion with an offset bulge but little outer optical distortion, making its interaction role plausible yet mixed.

quotes.quote: To the south, NGC 5474 is often thought to be the interacting companion driving M101's asymmetry, due to its own asymmetric nature ( Kornreich 1998) and the presence of diffuse HI between the galaxies (Huchtmeier Witzel 1979; Mihos 2012). However, NGC 5474's outer isophotes are remarkably round, with no distortion at large radius indicating any strong tidal interaction. This lack of irregular isophotes at large radius is particularly noteworthy given the significant offset (1.25 kpc, or 0.3 25) of NGC 5474's bulge from disk of the galaxy (van der Hulst Huchtmeier 1979; Kornreich 1998). Because the lifetime of an offset nucleus should be short, on the order of a few dynamical times ( a few 10^8 Myr), this argues that any interaction driving this morphology is ongoing or recent.

Another possible interaction partner is NGC 5474, projected 44 \ (88 kpc) to the south, but the evidence here is mixed as well. NGC 5474 is more luminous than NGC 5477, making it likely more able to drive a stronger response in M101, but the luminosity ratio between it and M101 (17:1 in V-band) would still classify it as a low mass encounter.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5474 as the larger southern companion whose lopsided morphology, diffuse H I, and projected separation make it the likelier recent perturber of M101.

quotes.quote: A second candidate is the larger and more luminous companion NGC 5474, located 44 \ (88 kpc) to the south of M101. NGC 5474's lopsided morphology suggests it too has suffered a recent interaction, and extremely diffuse HI is seen between it and the larger M101 (Huchtmeier Witzel 1979, Mihos 2012). Nonetheless its distance from M101 and the lack of obvious tidal features even at faint surface brightness (M+13) make the direct connection to M101's dynamical history still somewhat circumstantial.

In projection, the separation could be significantly smaller, of course, but the rough agreement between the expected separation and NGC 5474's projected distance from M101 (88 kpc) lends support to a interaction scenario involving these two galaxies. In summary, we are left with a picture of M101 where a recent interaction, likely with the companion galaxy NGC 5474, has stirred up gas in the outer disk and triggered a weak burst of star formation 300-400 Myr ago, which is now fading out.

sources.summary: NGC 5474 is described as a highly asymmetric late-type peculiar dwarf in the M101 group, likely interacting with M101 and very probably associated with FXRT 4.

quotes.quote: FXRT 4/XRT 071203 (cataloged as an X-ray source by Mineo2012 and Wang2016 , although never classified as an FXRT) is located in the SA(s)cd peculiar dwarf galaxy NGC 5474 ( m_ R = 10.8 AB mag; 5.9 Mpc), at an angular (projected) distance of 0 4 ( 0.7 kpc). NGC 5474 is a highly asymmetric late-type peculiar dwarf galaxy in the M101 group, thought to be interacting with M101. The probability of this occurring by chance is 1.9 10 ^ 6 based on its angular offset and the space density of m_ R < 10.8 AB mag galaxies, thus implying a highly probable association; the binomial probability that this FXRT is a background source is 9.9 10 ^ 4 , reinforcing an association with NGC 5474 (see Sect. sec:population for more details).

gmw_00002092.name: NGC 5477
gmw_00002092.overview: NGC 5477 is a low-luminosity dwarf irregular companion galaxy located near the giant spiral galaxy M101 [arXiv:2109.06430, arXiv:1806.06828]. The galaxy is clearly distorted and interacting with M101, though its low mass makes it unlikely to have driven M101's global disk asymmetry alone [arXiv:1210.8333, arXiv:1806.06828].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5477 as M101’s low-luminosity dwarf companion near the northwestern arm, with a velocity field blended with M101 and resembling newly detected clouds.

quotes.quote: The two closest companions to M101 are NGC 5477 and NGC 5474. NGC 5477 resides near the northwestern spiral arm of M101, while NGC 5474 with a strongly asymmetric disk Rownd1994,Kornreich1998 is located to the south Mihos2012 . Because NGC 5477 has a low luminosity ( M_ V =-15.3), it can't be massive enough to creat such a strong morphological response in a giant Sc spiral like M101 Mihos2012 .

The velocity field of the cloud C2 very similar to the dwarf companion NGC 5477 shows an irregular velocity gradient. From the position-velocity diagrams in panels -e of Figure Fig:M101-PV , we also see that the clouds C1, C2, and C3 similar to NGC 5477 rotate with the gas disk of M101. Unlike the companion NGC 5477, however, they have no optical counterparts.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5477 as M101's distorted dwarf companion, redshifted relative to M101 and interacting with it, but probably too low-luminosity to cause M101's strongest asymmetry alone.

quotes.quote: Several dynamical models have been proposed to explain this high velocity gas, including infall of gas clouds onto M101's disk (vdHS88) and tidal perturbations due to the interaction with M101's dwarf companion galaxy NGC 5477 (Combes 1991). In slice C, NGC 5477 can be seen at the beginning of the cut at =330 , redshifted 50 \ with respect to M101. The two most obvious candidates are NGC 5477, located 22 (44 kpc) east of M101, and NGC 5474, 44 (88 kpc) to the south. NGC 5477 resides near M101's crooked NE spiral arm; while it is clearly distorted and interacting with M101, its low luminosity (M _V =-15.3 ; de Vaucouleurs 1991) makes it unlikely to be massive enough to produce such a strong morphological response in a giant Sc spiral like M101. With growing evidence that both NGC 5477 and NGC 5474 have recently interacted with M101, we are building a picture of the M101 group as a dynamically active system.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 5477 as a dwarf companion positioned between M101’s outer features, possibly exciting disk structure but likely too low-luminosity to explain the full disturbance.

quotes.quote: The dwarf companion NGC 5477 sits between these two features, in a gap reminiscent of those seen in simulations of disk galaxies interacting with dwarf companions ( Hernquist Mihos 1995; Walker 1996).

One obvious culprit is the dwarf companion NGC 5477, which currently sits 22 \ (44 kpc) east of M101, between the NE Plume and E Spur. Indeed, the morphology of these features is similar to that expected when a satellite galaxy on a prograde orbit opens a gap in the galactic disk ( Hernquist Mihos 1995; Walker 1995). This could explain the structure of the low surface brightness outer disk, and also perhaps the high velocity gas seen associated with the features, as a small companion embedded in the outer disk could easily excite non-planar motions. However, it would seem difficult for a low luminosity dwarf like NGC 5477 to drive such a strong response in the inner regions of a massive Sc spiral like M101 as well.

sources.summary: The paper considers NGC 5477 as a nearby dwarf irregular companion, but argues its low luminosity and timing make it unlikely to have driven M101's global outer-disk disturbance.

quotes.quote: One possibility is the dwarf irregular galaxy NGC 5477, projected only 22 \ (44 kpc) away from the center of M101. While its proximity is favorable for driving a local response in the galaxy's outer disk, it is difficult to see how such a low luminosity dwarf could drive the strong global response of M101's asymmetric disk.

The timescale argument also works against scenarios involving the smaller NGC 5477 --- while the dwarf is projected close to the NE Plume today , if the encounter happened 300 Myr ago as traced by the burst population, then given M101's rotation speed, the outer disk has gone through one-third of a rotation since the burst was triggered. The observed proximity of NGC 5477 to the NE Plume may thus merely be happenstance, with the dwarf promoting only a weak perturbation at best on M101's already damaged outer disk.

gmw_00002093.name: HATLASJ113526.2-01460
gmw_00002093.overview: HATLASJ113526.2-01460 is an optical- and near-infrared-dark dusty starburst galaxy at redshift z ≈ 3.1 [arXiv:2207.00466]. The galaxy is strongly lensed by a foreground lens [arXiv:2207.00466] and features extremely compact star-forming and gas regions with high star formation rates [arXiv:2207.00466].

sources.summary: The paper resolves HATLASJ113526.2-01460 as an optical/NIR-dark strongly lensed z≈3.1 dusty starburst with compact emission, high star formation, and large gas and dust masses.

quotes.quote: ALMA resolves the first strongly-lensed Optical/NIR-dark galaxy 0000-0002-1847-4496 Marika Giulietti SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy INAF - Osservatorio di Astrofisica e Scienza dello Spazio, Via Gobetti 93/3, I-40129, Bologna, Italy 0000-0002-4882-1735 Andrea Lapi SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy IFPU - Institute for fundamental physics of the Universe, Via Beirut 2, 34014 Trieste, Italy INFN-Sezione di Trieste, via Valerio 2, 34127 Trieste, Italy INAF/IRA, Istituto di Radioastronomia, Via Piero Gobetti 101, 40129 Bologna, Italy 0000-0002-0375-8330 Marcella Massardi INAF/IRA, Istituto di Radioastronomia, Via Piero Gobetti 101, 40129 Bologna, Italy INAF, Istituto di Radioastronomia - Italian ARC, Via Piero Gobetti 101, I-40129 Bologna, Italy SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy 0000-0002-6444-8547 Meriem Behiri SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy 0000-0001-9301-5209 Martina Torsello SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy 0000-0002-9948-0897 Quirino D'Amato SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy INAF - Osservatorio di Astrofisica e Scienza dello Spazio, Via Gobetti 93/3, I-40129, Bologna, Italy 0000-0002-3515-6801 Tommaso Ronconi SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy IFPU, Via Beirut 2, I-34014 Trieste, Italy INFN-Sezione di Trieste, via Valerio 2, I-34127 Trieste, Italy 0000-0001-9808-0843 Francesca Perrotta SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy 0000-0002-7922-8440 Alessandro Bressan SISSA, Via Bonomea 265, I-34136 Trieste, Italy We present high-resolution ( 0.1 arcsec) ALMA observations of the strongly-lensed galaxy HATLASJ113526.2-01460 at redshift z 3.1 discovered in the Gama 12 ^ th field of the Herschel ATLAS survey. The gravitationally lensed system is remarkably peculiar in that neither the background source nor the foreground lens show a clearly detected optical/NIR emission. We perform accurate lens modeling and source morphology reconstruction in three different (sub-)mm continuum bands, and in the C [ II ] and CO(8-7) spectral lines.

Our main findings are summarized below: The lens modeling indicates that the foreground lens is constituted by a (likely elliptical) galaxy with mass 10^ 11 M_ at z 1.5 , while the source is found to be an optical/NIR dark, dusty star-forming galaxy whose (sub-)mm continuum and line emissions are amplified by factors 6-13 . The emission of J1135 is extremely compact, with sizes 0.5 kpc for the star-forming region and 1 kpc for the gas component, with no clear evidence of rotation or of ongoing merging events. J1135 features a very high star-formation rate 10^3 M_ yr ^ 1 , that given the compact sizes is on the verge of the Eddington limit for starbursts. The radio luminosity at 6 cm from available EVLA observations is consistent with the star-formation activity, so that no significant contribution from a central AGN is emerging (see also Vishwas2018 ). J1135 is found to be extremely rich in gas 10^ 11 M_ and dust 10^9 M_ .

gmw_00002094.name: PKS J044-00
gmw_00002094.overview: PKS J044-00 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=0.845 [arXiv:2301.09999]. It is detected up to the GeV band by Fermi/LAT and contributes to a blended INTEGRAL hard X-ray source [arXiv:2301.09999].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS J044-00 as a z=0.845 flat-spectrum radio quasar contributing to a blended INTEGRAL source and detected by Fermi/LAT.

quotes.quote: PKS 0440-00 and 1RXS J044229.8-001823 In the right panel of Fig. a1 we show a very peculiar case as two blazars fall within the IBIS error circle: PKS J044-00 also known as QSO B0440-003 ( 1), a flat spectrum radio quasar at z=0.845 and 1RXS J044229.8-001823, also known as 2MASS J04423023-0018294 ( 2), a BL Lac object at z=0.449. For this reason it is incorrect to quote PKS 0044-00 as the only INTEGRAL detection as listed in 2022MNRAS.510.4796K survey catalogue; we suggest instead that this source should be called IGR J04426-0018, since its emission is the merger of two objects. The QSO is also detected up to the GeV band by Fermi/LAT and reported as 4FGL J0442.6-0017 ( 2020ApJ...892..105A , see dashed-dotted error ellipse in the figure) in the fourth AGN catalogue, while the BL Lac, originally indicated in the third Fermi catalogue as possible counterpart, has been excluded in latest one.
To estimate the 20-100 keV flux of each source, we assumed an equal share of emission as implied by the almost equal flux at soft X-ray energies and a similar spectral shape at X-ray energies.

gmw_00002095.name: 1RXS J044229.8-001823
gmw_00002095.overview: 1RXS J044229.8-001823 is a BL Lacertae object and high-synchrotron-peaked blazar located at a redshift of z=0.449 [arXiv:2301.09999]. Its high-energy emission is blended with that of the nearby flat spectrum radio quasar PKS J044-00 in INTEGRAL observations [arXiv:2301.09999].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1RXS J044229.8-001823 as a z=0.449 BL Lac blended with PKS J044-00 in INTEGRAL emission and listed as a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar.

quotes.quote: PKS 0440-00 and 1RXS J044229.8-001823 In the right panel of Fig. a1 we show a very peculiar case as two blazars fall within the IBIS error circle: PKS J044-00 also known as QSO B0440-003 ( 1), a flat spectrum radio quasar at z=0.845 and 1RXS J044229.8-001823, also known as 2MASS J04423023-0018294 ( 2), a BL Lac object at z=0.449. For this reason it is incorrect to quote PKS 0044-00 as the only INTEGRAL detection as listed in 2022MNRAS.510.4796K survey catalogue; we suggest instead that this source should be called IGR J04426-0018, since its emission is the merger of two objects.

However, the BL Lac object is listed in the 3HSP catalogue of high synchrotron-peaked blazars 2019A&A...632A..77C , showing a synchrotron peak at around 2 10 ^ 16 Hz. To estimate the 20-100 keV flux of each source, we assumed an equal share of emission as implied by the almost equal flux at soft X-ray energies and a similar spectral shape at X-ray energies.
